name: Back Squat (Barbell)
description: Set a barbell across the upper trapezius (high-bar) or posterior deltoids/scapular spine (low-bar), unrack, and stand with feet shoulder-width apart and toes turned out slightly. Brace the trunk, then descend by simultaneously flexing the hips and knees, keeping the bar over the midfoot and the spine in a neutral range, until the hip crease is at or below the top of the knee (or as depth allows). Drive back up by extending the hips and knees together to return to standing.

muscleRoles.notes: All four heads extend the knee; vasti are the dominant knee extensors throughout the range, with demand rising as depth increases.
Primary hip extensor; activation increases with squat depth and external load.
Posterior (ischiocondylar) fibers are a major hip extensor in deep hip flexion, contributing substantially out of the bottom position.
Biarticular action keeps length relatively constant during the squat; activation is modest compared with hip-hinge exercises but assists hip extension.

Controls forward tibial translation eccentrically/isometrically with the heel planted; in a heels-down squat the ankle returns toward neutral rather than actively plantarflexing, so its role is tibial stabilization rather than prime-mover plantarflexion.

Biarticular knee flexor and ankle plantarflexor that stabilizes the knee and ankle; it does not drive squat mechanics (the knee is extending), so it functions as a stabilizer rather than a plantarflexion synergist.
High isometric demand to maintain trunk extension against the flexion moment of the bar; demand is higher with low-bar and more trunk lean.
Segmental lumbar stabilization under load.
Frontal-plane lumbar stability.
Contributes to trunk bracing and intra-abdominal pressure.
Trunk bracing and control of trunk rotation/lateral flexion under the bar.
Trunk bracing and intra-abdominal pressure.
Intra-abdominal pressure generation during the brace and Valsalva.
Frontal-plane pelvis and knee control; resists hip adduction/knee valgus collapse.
Assists gluteus medius in frontal-plane hip stability.
Forms the bar shelf in the high-bar position and stabilizes the shoulder girdle isometrically.
Pulls the bar into the back and helps stiffen the trunk via thoracolumbar fascia tension.

cues.text: Push the floor away from you as you stand up.
Spread the floor apart with your feet so your knees track in line with your toes.
Take a big breath into your belly and brace your trunk before each rep, like preparing to be nudged.
Keep the bar traveling in a straight line over the middle of your foot.

regressions.name: Goblet Squat
regressions.description: Hold a single dumbbell or kettlebell at the chest; the anterior load counterbalances the hips and encourages an upright trunk, making depth and technique easier to learn with lighter absolute load.

regressions.name: Box Squat (to a bench or box)
regressions.description: Squat to a box set at a height that controls depth and gives a consistent target, reducing balance and depth demands while patterning the hip and knee bend.

progressions.name: Paused Back Squat
progressions.description: Add a 2-3 second pause in the bottom position to remove the stretch-shortening rebound, increasing time under tension and demand out of the hole at a given load.

progressions.name: Tempo Eccentric Back Squat
progressions.description: Use a slow 3-5 second lowering phase to increase eccentric demand and control, useful for hypertrophy and technique refinement before adding maximal loads.

dosing: Strength: 3-5 sets of 3-8 reps at roughly 70-85% 1RM, 2-3 sessions per week with at least 48 hours between heavy sessions; hypertrophy: 3-4 sets of 6-12 reps at moderate loads taken near failure. Consistent with general progressive resistance-training guidelines rather than squat-specific trials.
emgNotes: EMG literature on the barbell squat is extensive. Vastus lateralis and vastus medialis show high activation that increases with both depth and load, and the squat is among the highest quadriceps-activating compound lifts. Gluteus maximus activation rises meaningfully with deeper squats and heavier relative loads. Hamstring activation is modest (typically well below that seen in deadlifts or Nordic exercise) because the biarticular hamstrings stay near-constant length as hip and knee flex together. Modeling and EMG work indicate the adductor magnus is a substantial hip extensor contributor in deep hip flexion. Trunk extensors (erector spinae) work at high isometric intensities to resist the flexion moment, with greater demand in low-bar and more inclined-trunk styles. Stance width mainly shifts adductor and glute contribution, with wider stances tending to increase hip musculature involvement.

name: Front Squat
description: Rack a barbell across the front of the shoulders in a clean grip (elbows high, fingertips under the bar) or crossed-arm position. Squat down by bending the hips and knees while keeping the torso upright and elbows lifted, descending until the hips are at or below knee level, then drive back up to standing. The anterior bar position demands an upright trunk, making it more quadriceps-dominant and thoracic-extension intensive than the back squat.

muscleRoles.notes: Anterior bar placement and upright torso increase knee-extensor demand relative to the back squat; all four heads work concentrically on ascent and eccentrically on descent.
Primary hip extensor driving the ascent, most active out of the deep bottom position.
Acts as a powerful hip-extension synergist from deep hip flexion.
Function largely isometrically, co-contracting with the quadriceps to stabilize the knee; net length change across hip and knee is small.
High isometric demand to resist trunk flexion under the anterior load; a defining feature of the front squat.
Segmental lumbar stabilization against the flexion moment.
Co-contracts with the trunk extensors to brace intra-abdominal pressure.
Contributes to circumferential trunk bracing.
Frontal-plane pelvis and knee alignment; resists hip adduction/valgus collapse.
Supports the bar on the shoulder shelf and helps keep the rack position elevated.
Maintains shoulder flexion to keep the elbows high and the bar seated in the rack position.

Active ankle/tibial stabilizer: contracts eccentrically to control forward tibial translation on descent and shortens on ascent. Not a passively stretched antagonist, so 'lengthening' is inappropriate; adequate soleus extensibility remains a prerequisite for the deep, upright position.

cues.text: Drive your elbows up toward the ceiling for the whole rep — if the elbows drop, the bar rolls forward.
Push the floor away from you as you stand up.
Keep the bar traveling in a straight vertical line over the middle of your foot.
Brace your trunk 360 degrees before you descend, as if preparing to be poked in the stomach.

regressions.name: Goblet Squat
regressions.description: Hold a kettlebell or dumbbell at the chest. Preserves the anterior-load, upright-torso pattern with lighter load and no wrist/rack mobility demand.

regressions.name: Front Squat to Box
regressions.description: Squat to a box or bench set at or slightly above parallel to control depth and build confidence in the pattern before progressing to free depth.

progressions.name: Paused Front Squat
progressions.description: Add a 2-3 second pause at the bottom position to increase time under tension, positional strength, and trunk-bracing demand out of the hole.

progressions.name: Clean-Grip Front Squat with Heavier Load / Clean Complex
progressions.description: Progress load toward 80-90% 1RM for low-rep strength work, or pair with a power clean into front squat complex to add a dynamic catch demand.

dosing: For strength: 3-5 sets of 3-8 reps at roughly 70-85% 1RM, 2-3 sessions per week with at least 48 hours between sessions, consistent with general resistance-training guidelines for trained and untrained adults. For hypertrophy emphasis: 3-4 sets of 6-12 reps at moderate loads taken close to fatigue.
emgNotes: Published biomechanical comparisons of the front squat versus the back squat report broadly similar EMG activation of the quadriceps, hamstrings, and lumbar erector spinae despite the front squat being performed with meaningfully lower absolute loads, alongside lower tibiofemoral compressive forces and smaller knee extensor moments at equivalent relative intensities. This makes the front squat an efficient quadriceps stimulus per unit of spinal and knee compressive load. The upright trunk position shifts demand toward the thoracic extensors and anterior core to resist the forward-flexion moment of the anteriorly racked bar, and vastus activation tends to increase with squat depth. Hamstring activity is modest and largely stabilizing rather than prime-moving.

name: Goblet Squat
description: Hold a kettlebell or dumbbell vertically against your chest, cupping it with both hands under the horns or bell, feet shoulder-width or slightly wider with toes turned out 10-30 degrees. Squat down by bending the hips and knees together, keeping the trunk tall and the weight glued to the chest, until the elbows pass inside the knees or you reach your comfortable depth, then drive through the whole foot to stand back up. The anterior load acts as a counterbalance, making it easier to stay upright and reach depth than in an unloaded or back-loaded squat.

muscleRoles.notes: Controls knee flexion eccentrically on the descent and produces knee extension on the ascent; demand increases with squat depth.
Primary hip extensor out of the bottom position; activation increases with depth and load.
Acts as a powerful hip extensor from deep hip flexion, especially in a wider stance.
Assist hip extension; activation is modest in squatting compared with hinge patterns because they shorten at the knee while lengthening at the hip.
Controls forward tibial translation as the ankle dorsiflexes in the descent and assists ankle extension on the ascent.
Isometrically resists trunk flexion against the anterior load; the upright goblet position keeps extensor moments lower than a comparable back squat.
Segmental lumbar stabilization during loaded trunk control.
Anterior loading raises abdominal bracing demand relative to back-loaded squats.
Contributes to trunk bracing and resisting rib flare with the load held at the chest.
Deep abdominal bracing to stiffen the trunk under load.
Frontal-plane control of the pelvis and femur, resisting knee valgus collapse.
Isometrically supports the load held in front of the chest.
Isometric elbow flexion to cradle the bell against the chest.
Supports the shoulder girdle under the anterior load.

cues.text: Push the floor away from you as you stand up.
Keep the bell glued to your breastbone the whole rep — do not let it drift away from you.
At the bottom, let your elbows track just inside your knees and gently pry the knees out over the toes.
Sit down between your heels, not back behind you — chest stays tall.

regressions.name: Goblet box squat
regressions.description: Squat to a box or bench of a height that matches the patient's controllable depth; the target reduces balance and depth demands and builds confidence in the pattern.

regressions.name: Bodyweight counterbalance squat
regressions.description: Squat holding a light plate or arms reaching forward as a counterweight, or holding a suspension strap/doorframe, to groove depth and an upright trunk before adding a goblet load.

progressions.name: Double kettlebell front rack squat
progressions.description: Two kettlebells in the front rack position markedly increase total load and anterior core/upper back demand while keeping the front-loaded, upright pattern.

progressions.name: Barbell front squat
progressions.description: Barbell in the front rack allows progressive loading well beyond what can be held at the chest, preserving the upright-trunk, quad-dominant characteristics of the goblet squat.

dosing: For strength/hypertrophy in novice-to-intermediate trainees, consistent with general resistance-training guidelines: 2-4 sets of 8-12 repetitions at a moderate load (leaving 2-3 reps in reserve), 2-3 sessions per week; as a movement-prep or motor-control drill, 1-2 lighter sets of 5-8 slow, deep repetitions before lower-body training.
emgNotes: Goblet-squat-specific EMG studies are sparse; activation inferences come mainly from the broader squat literature. That literature consistently shows high vasti (quadriceps) activation that rises with squat depth and load, gluteus maximus activation that increases with depth, and comparatively modest hamstring activity because the hamstrings shorten at the knee while lengthening at the hip. Front-loaded squat variants shift the trunk more upright than back squats, which tends to lower lumbar extensor moment and shear while increasing anterior trunk (abdominal) bracing demand; the goblet position adds sustained isometric work for the anterior deltoid, elbow flexors, and upper trapezius to support the load. Adductor magnus contributes substantially to hip extension from deep flexion, particularly in wider stances.
unmappedWanted: squat / triple-extension composite movement pattern (roster movements are single-joint only)

name: Barbell Bench Press
description: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor, and shoulder blades retracted and depressed into the bench. Unrack the bar with a grip slightly wider than shoulder width, lower it under control to the mid-chest, then press it back up until the elbows are extended, keeping the wrists stacked over the elbows and a slight backward bar path toward the rack.
bodyPosition: supine on flat bench, feet on floor

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover for shoulder horizontal adduction; both sternal and clavicular heads heavily loaded, lengthening eccentrically during the controlled descent.
Assists horizontal adduction and shoulder flexion throughout the press.
Drives elbow extension; contribution rises near lockout and with narrower grips (arguably a co-primary mover, but secondary is acceptable).
Helps control scapular position against the bench during the press.
Maintains the retracted scapular set that provides a stable base.
Works with the rhomboids to hold scapular retraction.
Rotator cuff co-contraction centers the humeral head in the glenoid under load.
Anterior cuff stabilization of the glenohumeral joint.
Long head acts as a dynamic anterior glenohumeral stabilizer, especially in the bottom position; a marginal but anatomically defensible inclusion.
Isometric tension controls the descent and helps set the shoulder position/bar path.
Maintains the modest thoracolumbar arch and trunk rigidity during leg drive.
Isometric contribution to leg drive with hips kept on the bench.
Antagonist placed on relative stretch as the humerus horizontally adducts.

cues.text: Pull your shoulder blades back and down and pin them into the bench before you unrack.
Touch the bar to mid-chest, then push it up and slightly back toward the rack.
Keep your wrists stacked over your elbows with the bar sitting in the heel of your palm.
Drive your feet into the floor without letting your hips lift off the bench.

regressions.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
regressions.description: Lighter absolute load per hand and freer shoulder path; useful for building pressing capacity and addressing side-to-side asymmetry before loading a fixed bar.

regressions.name: Push-Up (or Wall/Incline Push-Up)
regressions.description: Closed-chain horizontal press scaled by body angle; builds the same pressing pattern with lower joint load and no equipment or spotter demands.

progressions.name: Paused Bench Press
progressions.description: Add a 1-2 second pause with the bar on the chest to remove the stretch-shortening rebound, increasing time under tension and bottom-position strength.

progressions.name: Close-Grip Bench Press
progressions.description: Narrow the grip to roughly shoulder width to increase elbow-extension demand and triceps loading while keeping the same movement pattern.

dosing: Strength: 3-5 sets of 3-6 reps at approximately 80-90% 1RM, 2-3 sessions per week with at least 48 hours between sessions. Hypertrophy: 3-4 sets of 6-12 reps at 65-80% 1RM taken near (but not necessarily to) failure. These ranges follow general resistance-training guidelines (e.g., ACSM position stands) applied to the bench press.
emgNotes: The bench press is one of the most studied resistance exercises. Surface EMG work consistently shows high activation of the pectoralis major (both heads), anterior deltoid, and triceps brachii, with pectoralis major generally the dominant contributor on a flat bench. Incline variations shift relative activation toward the clavicular pec fibers and anterior deltoid, while decline and flat benches favor the sternocostal fibers. Narrower grips increase triceps activation and wider grips modestly increase pec activation but raise anterior shoulder stress. The sticking region occurs shortly after the chest, where mechanical leverage is poorest. Rotator cuff and scapular muscles show meaningful co-contraction for glenohumeral stability, which is why a retracted, depressed scapular set is standard coaching.
unmappedWanted: pectoralis-minor (scapular stabilizer during pressing; not in muscle roster)
coracobrachialis (assists horizontal adduction; not in muscle roster)

name: Dumbbell Bench Press
description: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor, holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest level with wrists stacked over elbows. Press both dumbbells upward until the elbows are extended (without locking out forcefully), then lower under control until the upper arms reach roughly bench level or a comfortable stretch across the chest. Keep the shoulder blades gently set back and down against the bench throughout.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover of the dumbbell bench press; sternal and clavicular fibers drive shoulder horizontal adduction and flexion through the press.
Strong synergist assisting shoulder flexion and horizontal adduction; activation is high but it is not THE prime mover — downgraded from primary.
Extends the elbow to complete the press; demand somewhat lower with dumbbells than with a barbell.
Controls scapular position and contributes to protraction/upward rotation at the top of the press.
Rotator cuff co-contraction centers the humeral head; demand rises with independent dumbbell loads.
Anterior cuff stabilization of the glenohumeral joint under load.
Assists humeral head compression and centering during the press.
Posterior cuff support against anterior humeral translation at the bottom position.
Maintains scapular set against the bench, providing a stable pressing platform.
Holds scapular retraction isometrically during the movement.
Antagonist to horizontal adduction; lengthens as the dumbbells are pressed together over the chest.
Antagonist to shoulder flexion; elongates modestly as the arms press up and slightly overhead of the chest line.

cues.text: Set your shoulder blades back and down into the bench before the first rep and keep them there.
Press the dumbbells up and slightly together, as if drawing two lines toward one point above your chest.
Lower with control until you feel a comfortable stretch across your chest — do not let the weights drop below your control.
Drive your feet into the floor for a stable base without lifting your hips off the bench.

regressions.name: Dumbbell Floor Press
regressions.description: Perform the press lying on the floor; the floor limits shoulder extension range, reducing stretch on the anterior shoulder and making load easier to control.

regressions.name: Push-Up (Wall or Incline)
regressions.description: Closed-chain horizontal press at reduced bodyweight load; builds the same pressing pattern with lower stability and strength demands.

progressions.name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Bench Press
progressions.description: Press one dumbbell at a time; the asymmetric load substantially increases trunk anti-rotation and shoulder stabilizer demand.

progressions.name: Tempo or Incline Dumbbell Bench Press
progressions.description: Add a 3-4 second eccentric or move to a 30-45 degree incline to increase time under tension or bias the clavicular pectoralis and anterior deltoid.

dosing: General strengthening: 2-4 sets of 8-12 repetitions, 2-3 sessions per week on nonconsecutive days, at a load allowing 1-3 reps in reserve (consistent with ACSM resistance-training guidance). For maximal strength emphasis: 3-5 sets of 4-6 repetitions at higher loads with 2-3 minutes rest.
emgNotes: The bench press is among the most-studied resistance exercises. Surface EMG work consistently shows highest activation in the pectoralis major and anterior deltoid, with the triceps brachii as the main synergist. Comparative studies of dumbbell versus barbell bench pressing generally report similar or slightly higher pectoralis major activity with dumbbells, lower triceps activity (a narrower, freer pressing path reduces elbow-extension demand), and greater demand on glenohumeral and scapular stabilizers because each arm must control its load independently, which also typically limits absolute load to below barbell equivalents. Incline positioning shifts emphasis toward the clavicular pectoralis and anterior deltoid.

name: Barbell Row
description: Stand with feet hip-width apart, hinge at the hips to roughly 30-45 degrees of trunk lean (or near-parallel for the strict variation) with a neutral spine, and grip the barbell just outside the knees. Pull the bar to the lower ribs/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades together, then lower under control to full elbow extension without letting the trunk or shoulders round forward.

muscleRoles.notes: Main humeral extensor pulling the arm from flexion back toward the trunk against the load.
Drives scapular retraction at the top of the pull.
Retracts and stabilizes the scapula throughout the concentric phase.
Contributes to shoulder extension and horizontal abduction, especially with a wider, more flared elbow position.
Assists latissimus dorsi in shoulder extension and adduction.
Elbow flexor loaded during the pull; more involved with a supinated grip.
Elbow flexion regardless of forearm position.
Elbow flexion, prominent with the neutral-to-pronated grip.
Assists scapular retraction and resists scapular elevation/anterior tilt.
Posterior rotator cuff centers the humeral head in the glenoid and resists internal rotation during the pull (dynamic stabilization, not a prime mover).
Works with infraspinatus to center the humeral head in the glenoid throughout the lift.
High isometric demand maintaining the neutral hip-hinged trunk against gravity and the load; often the limiting factor.
Segmental lumbar stabilization in the sustained hinge.
Frontal-plane trunk stability, resisting side-to-side sway.
Isometric hip extension holding the hinge position.
Isometric hip extension and posterior pelvic control in the hinge; loaded in a lengthened position.
Grip on the bar.
Grip on the bar.
Intra-abdominal bracing to protect the lumbar spine.
Co-contracts with spinal extensors during bracing to resist trunk momentum.
Anterior chest lengthens as the shoulders extend and the scapulae retract at the top of the pull.
Stretched antagonist to shoulder extension at end range of the pull.

cues.text: Pull the bar to your lower ribs, like starting a lawnmower with both hands.
Squeeze an imaginary pencil between your shoulder blades at the top of each rep.
Brace your abs and keep your back flat, chest proud, before every pull.
Keep the bar close, sliding it up your thighs, and do not let your torso bounce up to lift it.

regressions.name: Chest-Supported Dumbbell Row
regressions.description: Perform the row prone on an incline bench so the bench supports the trunk, removing the spinal-loading and hip-hinge demand while training the same pulling pattern.

regressions.name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row (Bench-Supported)
regressions.description: One knee and hand on a bench provide three points of support, reducing lumbar load and allowing focus on scapular retraction and shoulder extension one side at a time.

progressions.name: Pendlay Row
progressions.description: Row from a dead stop on the floor each rep with the torso near parallel, eliminating stretch reflex and increasing demand on the upper back and spinal erectors.

progressions.name: Deficit or Paused Barbell Row
progressions.description: Add a 2-3 second pause with the bar at the ribs (or stand on a low platform to increase range of motion) to increase time under tension and scapular retraction endurance.

dosing: General resistance-training guidelines: 2-4 sets of 6-12 repetitions at ~65-85% 1RM, 2-3 sessions per week for hypertrophy/strength; lower reps (4-6) at higher loads for strength emphasis. No condition-specific dosing evidence for this exercise beyond general strength-training recommendations.
emgNotes: Published EMG comparisons of rowing variations show the bent-over barbell row produces high activation of the latissimus dorsi, middle trapezius, rhomboids, and posterior deltoid, with substantial isometric activity in the lumbar erector spinae — spine-load analyses report notably higher lumbar compression and shear in the free-standing bent-over row than in inverted or chest-supported rows, which is clinically relevant for low-back-sensitive patients. Biceps brachii and brachioradialis activity is meaningful and increases with a supinated grip; a more flared elbow shifts emphasis toward the posterior deltoid and horizontal abductors, while elbows closer to the trunk emphasize the latissimus dorsi. The upper trapezius activates to resist scapular depression/protraction under load but should not dominate the movement.

name: Bent-Over Row
description: Stand holding a barbell or dumbbells, hinge at the hips to roughly 30-45 degrees of trunk inclination with a neutral spine and soft knees, and let the arms hang. Pull the load toward the lower ribs/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades together, then lower under control to full elbow extension without losing the hinge position.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover for glenohumeral extension/adduction, drawing the humerus back toward the trunk
Scapular retraction through the pull
Scapular retraction and downward-rotation control with middle trapezius
Shoulder extension/horizontal abduction, especially with a wider high-elbow path
Assists latissimus in shoulder extension and adduction

Assists retraction and resists shrugging during the pull
Posterior rotator cuff centering the humeral head during loaded extension
Glenohumeral stabilization with infraspinatus
High isometric demand holding the hinged trunk against the load moment arm
Segmental lumbar stabilization in the hinge
Isometric hip-extension torque maintaining the hinge position
Isometric hip control holding the hinge
Trunk bracing/intra-abdominal pressure in the hinged position
Isometric grip on the bar or dumbbells
Isometric grip on the bar or dumbbells

cues.text: Hinge at the hips and push your hips back until your torso is about 30-45 degrees over the floor, chest proud, spine long.
Imagine squeezing an orange between your shoulder blades as you pull the weight to your lower ribs.
Drive your elbows toward the back pockets rather than curling with the hands.
Keep the torso angle frozen — if you have to heave upright to finish the rep, the load is too heavy.

regressions.name: Chest-Supported Dumbbell Row
regressions.description: Perform the row prone on an incline bench so the bench, not the spine and hips, resists the trunk moment — removes the hinge-endurance demand and lumbar load.

regressions.name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row (Bench-Supported)
regressions.description: One knee and hand on a bench, row a dumbbell with the free arm; the tripod support reduces spinal loading and lets the lifter learn the pull pattern unilaterally.

progressions.name: Pendlay Row
progressions.description: Row a barbell from a dead stop on the floor each rep with a near-horizontal torso — increases range of motion, eliminates stretch-shortening assistance, and raises trunk-stability demand.

progressions.name: Heavy Barbell Row with Tempo Eccentric
progressions.description: Increase load toward 6RM range and add a 3-4 second controlled lowering phase to increase mechanical tension on the pulling musculature and hinge stabilizers.

dosing: For strength: 2-4 sets of 6-12 repetitions, 2-3 sessions per week, consistent with general resistance-training guidelines (e.g., ACSM progression recommendations); progress load when all reps are completed with a stable trunk position.
emgNotes: Surface EMG comparisons of rowing variants (e.g., work from McGill's group comparing bent-over, inverted, and one-arm cable rows) show the bent-over row produces high activation of the latissimus dorsi, middle trapezius, and scapular retractors, together with substantial isometric thoracolumbar erector spinae activity and among the highest lumbar spine compressive loads of common rowing exercises because the trunk must be held cantilevered against the external load. Elbow flexor (biceps brachii/brachialis) activity is moderate and grip-dependent, and posterior deltoid contribution increases as the elbow path widens toward horizontal abduction. Chest-supported variants show similar upper-back activation with markedly lower spinal loading, which is the basis for using them as a regression.

serratus-posterior-superior

name: Lat Pulldown
description: Sit at a cable pulldown station with thighs secured under the pad, grasp the bar with a pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder width, and lean back about 10-15 degrees with the chest lifted. Pull the bar down to the upper chest by driving the elbows down and back while depressing the shoulder blades, then control the bar back to full overhead arm extension without letting the shoulders shrug or the torso swing.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover: shoulder adduction/extension from the overhead position. Correct primary and namesake target.
Strong assister in shoulder adduction/extension alongside the lat.
Elbow flexor in the pull; contribution rises with supinated grips, lower with the pronated grip described here but still meaningful.
Grip-independent elbow flexor; workhorse elbow flexor with the pronated grip.
Elbow flexion, favored by the pronated/neutral grip used here.
Assists shoulder extension/horizontal abduction as the elbows drive down and back with the wide pronated grip.
Drives scapular depression during the pull; primary coaching cue ('shoulders down').
Assists scapular retraction in the bottom range.
Scapular retraction and downward rotation as the scapula depresses/downwardly rotates in the pull. Anatomically correct downward rotator.
Long head assists shoulder extension/adduction at the glenohumeral joint despite the elbow flexing; minor but anatomically valid contribution.
Posterior rotator-cuff control of the humeral head during loaded overhead pulling.
Posterior cuff; centers the humeral head with infraspinatus.
Resists lumbar extension and torso swing under load.
Maintains the slight trunk lean and upright spine against the pull.
Sternocostal fibers assist shoulder adduction/extension of the overhead arm (same reason pec is active in straight-arm pulldowns).
As the scapula depresses and downwardly rotates, the upper trap (elevator/upward rotator) lengthens; over-activity (shrug) is the key compensation to coach out.

cues.text: Pull the bar to your collarbone, not your head to the bar.
Drive your elbows down toward your back pockets.
Before you pull, slide your shoulder blades down away from your ears and keep them there.
Control the bar back up for a slow 2-3 count — don't let the weight stack yank your shoulders into a shrug.

regressions.name: Tall-Kneeling Band Pulldown
regressions.description: Anchor a resistance band overhead and perform the same pulldown pattern from tall kneeling; lighter, scalable resistance and easier scapular control for patients not ready for machine loads.

regressions.name: Assisted Straight-Arm Scapular Depression
regressions.description: At the pulldown station with light weight, keep elbows straight and practice only the scapular depression component ('scap pulldowns') to groove lower-trap engagement before adding the full pull.

progressions.name: Pull-Up / Assisted Pull-Up
progressions.description: Progress to closed-chain vertical pulling with band or machine assistance, then bodyweight, which demands greater trunk stabilization and full body-mass loading.

progressions.name: Single-Arm Kneeling Pulldown
progressions.description: Perform the pulldown one arm at a time from half-kneeling at a cable column, adding an anti-rotation trunk demand and exposing side-to-side strength asymmetries.

dosing: General strengthening: 2-4 sets of 8-12 repetitions at a moderate-to-heavy load (roughly 60-80% 1RM), 2-3 sessions per week, consistent with ACSM resistance-training guidelines; hypertrophy-focused blocks may use 3-4 sets of 8-15 reps taken near volitional fatigue.
emgNotes: Surface EMG studies of the lat pulldown consistently show the latissimus dorsi as the dominant mover, with substantial contributions from teres major, biceps brachii, and the middle/lower trapezius. Grip-width comparisons generally find that a medium-to-wide pronated grip produces equal or slightly greater latissimus activation than very wide or supinated grips, while supinated (underhand) grips shift more work to the biceps brachii. Pulling the bar to the front of the chest produces similar lat activation to behind-the-neck variants with less anterior shoulder stress, so the front pulldown is the standard clinical recommendation. Excessive torso lean converts the movement toward a row and increases posterior deltoid and mid-trap contribution.

emgNotes: Surface EMG studies of horizontal rowing variations consistently show high activation of the latissimus dorsi, middle trapezius, and rhomboid region, with meaningful biceps brachii and posterior deltoid contribution. Comparative work on rowing exercises (inverted row vs. bent-over row vs. standing cable row) indicates cable rows produce solid upper-back activation with lower lumbar spine loading than free-weight bent-over rows, making the seated cable row a common choice when spinal load must be moderated. Emphasizing full scapular retraction at end-range increases middle/lower trapezius activity, while allowing torso lean-back shifts work toward the lumbar erectors and reduces upper-back specificity. Grip orientation modestly shifts elbow-flexor emphasis (supinated favors biceps; neutral favors brachioradialis) without materially changing back activation.

emgNotes: Surface EMG studies of the pull-up consistently show the latissimus dorsi as the most highly activated muscle, with substantial contributions from the biceps brachii and brachialis (elbow flexion) and from the lower trapezius and infraspinatus during the scapular depression and pulling phases. Grip orientation shifts emphasis: the pronated pull-up biases the latissimus dorsi and lower trapezius relative to the supinated chin-up, which shows higher biceps brachii and pectoralis major activity. Trunk musculature (rectus abdominis, obliques) works isometrically to control the hanging body. These findings come from small-sample EMG comparisons of pull-up variants in trained adults; exact percentages of MVIC vary across studies and electrode placements, so they should be read as relative rankings rather than precise values.

Peak lat activity occurs through the mid-range of the pull; the dead-hang position loads the lat at long muscle length under full bodyweight distraction. Serratus anterior and the trapezius force couple show progressively greater activity as the arms approach full overhead elevation. Rotator cuff muscles work throughout to center the humeral head, which is one reason free-weight dumbbell pressing is often preferred over machine pressing in later-stage shoulder rehabilitation.

dosing: Common practice for strengthening/hypertrophy: 2-4 sets of 8-15 repetitions, 2-3 sessions per week, with load selected so the last 2-3 reps are challenging while form (no trunk swing or shrug) is maintained. emgNotes: Surface EMG comparisons of common shoulder exercises consistently show the lateral (middle) deltoid is highly active during dumbbell lateral raises — typically among the highest of common free-weight shoulder exercises for that head, generally exceeding its activation during overhead pressing. The supraspinatus is also substantially active, working with the deltoid throughout abduction rather than only in the first degrees. Anterior and posterior deltoid activity is moderate and shifts with the plane of the raise: moving into scaption biases the anterior deltoid, while staying behind the frontal plane biases the posterior fibers. Upper trapezius activity rises with the arm, particularly above roughly 60-80 degrees, and increases further if the lifter shrugs or uses momentum. Mechanically the exercise loads the deltoid most near shoulder height where the load's moment arm is longest, which is why cable variants (constant tension at the bottom) feel harder early in the range. No specific citations are attached; this summary reflects the general pattern of published EMG comparisons of deltoid exercises.

The rope finish position biases middle and lower trapezius over upper trapezius when the athlete avoids shrugging, which is why it is commonly programmed for scapular muscle balance in overhead athletes. These inferences should not be treated as exercise-specific EMG findings for the face pull.

Antagonist that lengthens through the concentric curl and controls the elbow eccentrically on the lowering phase.

cues.text: Hold the dumbbells like hammers — thumbs up, palms facing each other the whole time.
Imagine your elbows are pinned to your ribs with a strap; only your forearms move.
Bring the top of the dumbbell toward your shoulder without letting the weight tip forward or back.
Lower for a slow count of three until the arm is fully straight — no dropping the weight.

regressions.name: Seated Alternating Hammer Curl
regressions.description: Sit with back supported and curl one arm at a time with a lighter dumbbell; the seat eliminates trunk sway and alternating arms halves the systemic demand.

regressions.name: Band Hammer Curl
regressions.description: Stand on a resistance band and curl with a neutral grip; the ascending band resistance is lightest at the weakest joint angle and is easy to titrate for deconditioned or post-injury patients.

progressions.name: Cross-Body Hammer Curl
progressions.description: Curl the dumbbell across the torso toward the opposite shoulder, further biasing the brachioradialis and brachialis and allowing heavier loads.

progressions.name: Eccentric-Emphasis Hammer Curl
progressions.description: Use a heavier load, assist the concentric with the opposite hand or momentum, then perform a 4-5 second controlled lowering to overload the eccentric phase.

dosing: 2-4 sets of 8-12 repetitions at a moderate load (roughly 65-80% 1RM or 2-3 reps in reserve), 2-3 sessions per week, consistent with general resistance-training guidelines for hypertrophy and strength; no hammer-curl-specific dosing trials exist.
emgNotes: Surface EMG comparisons of curl grip positions indicate that a neutral (hammer) grip produces relatively higher brachioradialis activation and somewhat lower biceps brachii activation than a supinated grip, because the biceps loses mechanical advantage as a supinator-flexor when the forearm is held in mid-position. The brachialis, inserting on the ulna, flexes the elbow irrespective of forearm rotation and is strongly recruited in all curl variants. Wrist flexors and extensors co-contract isometrically to keep the wrist neutral, and finger flexors work isometrically for grip. Overall the direct EMG literature on hammer curls specifically is sparse; most inferences come from studies of elbow-flexion tasks at varying forearm rotations.

name: Triceps Pushdown
description: Stand facing a cable column with a straight bar or rope attachment set at upper-chest height. With elbows pinned to your sides and a slight forward hinge at the hips, extend the elbows fully to push the handle down toward the thighs, then control the return until the forearms rise to roughly 90 degrees of elbow flexion without letting the elbows drift forward.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover for elbow extension; all three heads contribute, with the long head relatively shortened at the shoulder in this arm-at-side position.
Assists elbow extension and stabilizes the humeroulnar joint, particularly at initiation and near lockout.
Works isometrically to keep the humerus at the side and resist the cable pulling the arm into flexion.
Isometric shoulder extension/depression torque to anchor the upper arm against the rising cable line of pull.
Maintains scapular depression so the movement is not substituted with shrugging or leaning.
Braces the trunk against the anterior-superior pull of the cable, especially at heavier loads.
Antagonist elbow flexor that lengthens under control through the pressing phase.
Antagonist elbow flexor lengthening as the elbow extends.

cues.text: Pin your elbows to your ribs — only the forearms should move.
Push the handle straight down to the floor and pause briefly at full lockout.
Keep the chest tall and shoulders down; do not shrug or lean onto the bar as you press.
Let the handle rise slowly on the way up, stopping when the forearms reach about parallel to the floor.

regressions.name: Band Triceps Pushdown
regressions.description: Anchor a light resistance band overhead (door anchor or pull-up bar) and perform the same movement; the ascending band resistance is lighter in the lengthened position and easier to control.

regressions.name: Half-Range Pushdown
regressions.description: Perform the pushdown only through the bottom half of the range (from 90 degrees to full extension) with lighter load, useful early in rehab for lateral elbow or triceps tendon irritability.

progressions.name: Single-Arm Rope Pushdown
progressions.description: Perform one arm at a time with a rope handle, adding an anti-rotation trunk demand and exposing side-to-side strength asymmetries.

progressions.name: Overhead Cable Triceps Extension
progressions.description: Face away from the column and extend the elbows with the arms overhead; this trains the long head at longer muscle lengths and increases the shoulder-stability demand.

dosing: For strength/hypertrophy in healthy adults, 2-4 sets of 8-15 repetitions at moderate-to-hard effort, 2-3 sessions per week, consistent with general resistance-training guidelines; no pushdown-specific dosing trials exist.
emgNotes: Surface EMG comparisons of common triceps exercises consistently show the pushdown produces high activation of the lateral and medial heads of the triceps brachii, with somewhat lower relative recruitment of the long head because the arm-at-side position keeps the biarticular long head shortened at the shoulder. Training studies comparing arm-at-side versus overhead elbow extension suggest greater long-head hypertrophy when the shoulder is flexed (long head at longer lengths), so the pushdown is best viewed as biased toward the monoarticular heads. Rope attachments allowing terminal wrist spread and full elbow lockout are commonly reported to increase peak activation near end range compared with a fixed straight bar, though differences between attachments are modest.

name: Skull Crusher (Lying Triceps Extension)
description: Lie supine on a flat bench holding an EZ-curl bar or dumbbells with arms extended over the chest, shoulders flexed to roughly 90 degrees. Keeping the upper arms still, bend only the elbows to lower the load under control toward the forehead or just behind the crown of the head, then extend the elbows back to the start without letting the elbows flare or drift.

muscleRoles.notes: All three heads drive elbow extension; the long head is loaded in a lengthened position because the shoulder is held in flexion, which is the defining feature of the skull crusher.
Assists elbow extension and helps stabilize the elbow, especially near terminal extension.
Isometrically holds the shoulder at ~90 degrees flexion so the upper arm stays fixed while only the elbow moves.
Clavicular fibers assist in stabilizing the humerus against the load; minor contribution.
Wrist flexors co-contract with extensors to keep the wrist neutral under the bar.
Contributes to wrist stabilization while gripping the bar.
Biarticular elbow flexor that lengthens across the elbow during the extension (lifting) phase as the antagonist.
Monoarticular elbow-flexor antagonist stretched through the extension phase.

cues.text: Imagine your upper arms are pillars set in concrete — only your forearms move.
Lower the bar toward the top of your head, then push it back up toward the ceiling, not toward your feet.
Keep your elbows pointing at the ceiling and shoulder-width apart; don't let them flare out.
Take 2-3 seconds to lower, then extend smoothly without bouncing out of the bottom.

regressions.name: Band or Cable Triceps Pressdown
regressions.description: Standing pressdown with a band or cable keeps the shoulder neutral and reduces elbow torque in the stretched position, making it easier to control for beginners or during tendon irritability.

regressions.name: Floor Skull Crusher with Light Dumbbells
regressions.description: Performing the movement on the floor limits the range of motion at the bottom (elbows can rest briefly), reducing peak stretch load and providing a built-in safety stop.

progressions.name: Incline or Behind-the-Head Skull Crusher
progressions.description: Increasing shoulder flexion (incline bench or lowering the bar behind the head) lengthens the triceps long head further, increasing the stretch-loaded stimulus.

progressions.name: Eccentric-Accentuated Skull Crusher
progressions.description: Use a heavier load or added band tension for a slow 4-5 second lowering phase, then assist or use both arms to return, emphasizing eccentric overload.

dosing: For hypertrophy: 2-4 sets of 8-12 repetitions at moderate load, 2-3 sessions per week, consistent with general resistance-training guidelines; for strength emphasis, 3-5 reps at higher load once technique is stable.
emgNotes: Surface EMG comparisons of common triceps exercises show the lying triceps extension produces moderate-to-high triceps brachii activation, though generally below weighted dips, close-grip pressing, or bodyweight extensions in some comparisons. Because the shoulder is held in flexion, the long head works at a longer muscle length than in pressdown variants, and recent longitudinal work on stretch-position training suggests long-length loading of the triceps (e.g., overhead or behind-the-head variants) may favor greater long-head hypertrophy. Wrist and shoulder stabilizer activity is modest and primarily isometric.
unmappedWanted: triceps-brachii-long-head (roster only has the combined triceps-brachii group)
coracobrachialis (minor shoulder stabilizer)

name: Reverse Lunge
description: From standing, step one foot backward and lower the back knee toward the floor until both knees reach roughly 90 degrees, keeping the trunk upright and weight centered over the front foot. Drive through the front foot to return to standing, then repeat on the same side or alternate legs. Compared with a forward lunge, the stepping-back pattern keeps the front shin more vertical, which many patients with anterior knee pain tolerate better.
equipment: support surface or dowel (optional balance assist)

muscleRoles.notes: Lead-leg knee extensors control descent eccentrically and drive the ascent.
Lead-leg hip extension during the ascent; loading increases with forward trunk lean or added weight.
Assist lead-leg hip extension and co-contract to stabilize the knee.
Posterior fibers assist lead-leg hip extension from the deep split position.
Controls frontal-plane pelvic drop and lead-leg femoral adduction/internal rotation.
Works with gluteus medius for lateral hip stability on the stance leg.
Maintains an upright, neutral trunk throughout the split position.
Anticipatory trunk stiffness during the single-leg-dominant transition.
Acts largely isometrically to control the lead tibia/shank angle over the foot; trailing leg only lightly touches down, so push-off contribution is minimal.
Stabilizes the lead ankle and shank angle in stance.
Trail-leg hip flexors are placed on stretch at the bottom position as the trailing hip extends, especially with a posterior pelvic tilt.
On the trailing leg, combined hip extension and knee flexion lengthens the two-joint rectus femoris; on the lead leg it works within the quadriceps group.

cues.text: Drop the back knee straight down toward the floor, like an elevator, not forward.
Push the floor away through the whole front foot to stand back up.
Keep the front shin close to vertical with the knee tracking over the second toe.
Stay tall through the chest with ribs stacked over the pelvis; avoid collapsing forward.

regressions.name: Supported reverse lunge
regressions.description: Hold a countertop, dowel, or suspension trainer for balance assistance while performing the same stepping pattern.

regressions.name: Partial-depth reverse lunge
regressions.description: Lower only through a comfortable partial range, or tap the back knee to a raised pad/box to limit depth and load.

progressions.name: Loaded reverse lunge
progressions.description: Add a goblet-held kettlebell or dumbbells at the sides; progress load while maintaining trunk and knee control.

progressions.name: Deficit reverse lunge
progressions.description: Stand on a low step or plate so the back foot lowers below the front-foot level, increasing hip flexion range and gluteal demand.

dosing: Consistent with general resistance-training guidelines: 2-4 sets of 8-12 repetitions per leg, 2-3 sessions per week, progressing load or depth as tolerated. For hypertrophy or strength emphasis, adjust load so the last 2-3 reps are challenging. No reverse-lunge-specific dosing trials exist; this is extrapolated from general lower-limb resistance training evidence.
emgNotes: Lunge-family exercises show high activation of the quadriceps (vastus lateralis and medialis) and gluteus maximus of the lead leg, with moderate hamstring and gluteus medius activity for knee and pelvic stabilization. Biomechanical comparisons of lunge variants indicate that stepping backward keeps the lead tibia more vertical and reduces anterior translation demand and patellofemoral joint stress relative to the forward lunge, shifting relative emphasis slightly toward the hip extensors. Trunk lean forward increases gluteal and hamstring contribution; an upright trunk biases the quadriceps. Most published EMG work examines forward or walking lunges, so reverse-lunge-specific activation data are sparse and largely inferred from the broader lunge literature.

name: Leg Extension
description: An open-kinetic-chain, single-joint knee extension performed on a leg extension machine. Sit tall with your back and hips against the pads, hips flexed to roughly 90 degrees, and hook the shins behind the padded roller so the pad rests just above the ankles. Align the machine's axis of rotation with the lateral knee joint line. Fasten the lap belt if available and hold the seat handles. Extend both knees smoothly until they are nearly straight (stopping short of a hard, hyperextended lockout), pause briefly with the quadriceps contracted, then lower under control back to the start. Keep the movement slow and deliberate rather than swinging the weight up.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover of open-chain knee extension; all four heads straighten the knee against the roller pad.
Large monoarticular knee extensor; heavily loaded throughout the range.
Monoarticular knee extensor; active through range and often emphasized in the terminal ~15-30 degrees for patellar tracking.
Deep monoarticular knee extensor working with the other vasti.

Biarticular quadriceps head and a direct knee extensor, but shortened/on slack at the flexed hip in the seated position (active insufficiency), so it contributes less than the vasti here. Reclassified from synergist to secondary because it is a reduced agonist within the prime-mover group, not an outside helper.
Antagonist group; biarticular hamstrings are stretched across the knee as it extends (hip fixed).
Biarticular hamstring stretched across the knee during extension.
Medial hamstring lengthened as the knee straightens.
Biarticular calf muscle crossing the posterior knee; placed on mild stretch across the knee as it extends.

cues.text: Sit tall with your back flat against the pad and line the knee joint up with the machine's pivot; the roller should rest just above the ankles, not on the shin bone.
Straighten the knees smoothly and squeeze the thighs at the top, stopping just short of a forced hyperextended lockout.
Lower the weight slowly (about 2-3 seconds) and stay in control instead of letting the plates drop or swinging your torso.
Exhale as you extend the knees and inhale as you lower; avoid gripping the seat so hard that you brace unnecessarily.

regressions.name: Terminal-range knee extension (short-arc)
regressions.description: Work only the last 15-30 degrees to full extension with lighter load. This shortens the lever arm and reduces patellofemoral stress, useful for early rehab or irritable knees.

regressions.name: Isometric quad set
regressions.description: Regress to a static seated quad contraction (or holding the leg at partial extension) with little or no external load to build tolerance before adding machine resistance through range.

progressions.name: Single-leg (unilateral) leg extension
progressions.description: Perform one leg at a time to increase per-limb load, expose side-to-side asymmetries, and add balance/stabilization demand.

progressions.name: Slow eccentric / tempo emphasis
progressions.description: Add a controlled 3-5 second lowering phase or a brief isometric hold at end range to raise time-under-tension and eccentric loading, or simply progress machine weight for strength/hypertrophy.

emgNotes: Surface EMG studies of open-chain knee extension consistently show high, well-balanced activation across the quadriceps heads. Contrary to older claims, the vastus medialis (VMO) cannot be selectively isolated from the rest of the quadriceps; VMO-to-vastus-lateralis activation ratios generally stay close to 1:1 across the range, though vastus medialis activity tends to be relatively higher in the terminal (near-full-extension) portion. Rectus femoris activation is somewhat blunted in the seated, hip-flexed position because the muscle is on relative slack. Compared with closed-chain movements like the squat, the open-chain leg extension isolates the quadriceps with minimal hamstring co-contraction. Note that peak patellofemoral joint compression occurs in the more flexed portion of the range while anterior tibiofemoral shear is greatest near terminal extension, which informs range selection in patellofemoral or ACL rehab.
dosing: General resistance-training guidelines apply, as no leg-extension-specific dosing is well established: for hypertrophy roughly 3-4 sets of 8-12 reps; for muscular endurance/early rehab 2-3 sets of 12-20 reps with lighter load; train the target 2-3 times per week with about 48 hours between sessions, progressing load as tolerated. In patellofemoral rehab, limiting range to the terminal ~45-0 degrees is commonly used to reduce joint stress.

name: Leg Curl
description: A single-joint knee-flexion exercise that isolates the hamstrings, performed on a machine (prone/lying or seated) or with a band/ankle weight. Set up so the movement axis of the machine aligns with the knee joint and the padded lever rests just above the heels/Achilles. Keeping the hips and pelvis pressed into the pad (avoid arching the low back or letting the hips rise), curl the heels toward the buttocks by flexing the knees through the available range. Pause briefly at end range, then lower under control back to near-full knee extension. Keep the ankles relaxed or lightly dorsiflexed and the toes neutral; breathe out on the curl and in on the return.
bodyPosition: Prone (lying) on a leg-curl machine bench, hips and torso supported; the seated leg-curl variation places the hip in flexion with the back against a pad.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover group for knee flexion throughout the range.
Lateral hamstring; biarticular knee flexor, placed on greater stretch (and higher force potential) in the seated/hip-flexed variation.
Medial hamstring; strong knee flexor.
Medial hamstring; strong knee flexor.
Uniarticular knee flexor; contributes regardless of hip position.
Crosses the posterior knee and assists knee flexion, especially with the ankle relaxed/plantarflexed.
Assists initiation of knee flexion by unlocking the extended knee.
Crosses the medial knee (pes anserinus) and assists flexion.
Assists knee flexion via its distal pes anserinus attachment.
In prone, contracts isometrically to keep the pelvis/hip from rising off the pad.
Stabilizes the lumbar spine to resist compensatory low-back arching during the curl.
Antagonist group that lengthens as the knee flexes and controls the eccentric lowering.
Biarticular quadriceps stretched further in the prone variation, where the hip is extended while the knee flexes.

cues.text: Press your hips and pelvis into the pad the whole time — the low back should not arch as you curl.
Drive your heels toward your glutes, then lower slowly and under control rather than letting the weight drop.
Keep the ankles relaxed and toes neutral so you flex from the knees, not by cramping the calves.
Match effort on both legs; watch for one heel curling faster or higher than the other.

regressions.name: Band or ankle-weight leg curl
regressions.description: Perform the same knee-flexion motion prone or standing against a light resistance band or ankle weight, reducing load so full control is maintained.

regressions.name: Partial-range / lighter-load machine curl
regressions.description: Use a lighter machine load and work through a comfortable mid-range, gradually extending range and load as strength and tolerance improve.

progressions.name: Eccentric-emphasis / slow-tempo curl
progressions.description: Lift with both legs (or normally) and lower on one leg or over a 3-5 second eccentric to increase hamstring tension and eccentric strength.

progressions.name: Single-leg leg curl
progressions.description: Perform the curl one leg at a time to expose and correct side-to-side strength asymmetries and increase relative load.

dosing: General resistance-training guidelines apply: for hypertrophy/general strength, 2-4 sets of 8-15 reps, 2-3 sessions per week with progressive overload and roughly 1-2 minutes rest between sets. No leg-curl-specific dosing protocol is established.
emgNotes: Surface-EMG studies of the leg curl consistently show high hamstring activation with position-dependent differences. Hip position shifts the emphasis: the seated (hip-flexed) leg curl tends to elicit greater overall hamstring EMG — particularly of the biarticular heads (biceps femoris long head, semitendinosus, semimembranosus) — because the hamstrings are placed on greater stretch, whereas the prone (hip-extended) curl still recruits the uniarticular biceps femoris short head robustly. Medial (semitendinosus/semimembranosus) versus lateral (biceps femoris) balance can be biased by tibial/foot rotation, and the gastrocnemius contributes as a secondary knee flexor. Reported magnitudes vary widely across studies and setups, so these are qualitative trends rather than fixed percentages.

name: Hip Thrust
description: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a bench, knees bent and feet flat about hip-width apart. Drive through the heels to lift the hips until the trunk and thighs are parallel to the floor, finishing with a strong glute squeeze and a slight posterior pelvic tilt, then lower under control. Keep the chin tucked and ribs down so the movement comes from the hips rather than the lumbar spine.
bodyPosition: supine-inclined (upper back supported on bench, feet on floor)

muscleRoles.notes: Main hip extensor; activation peaks at terminal hip extension at the top of the movement.
Assist hip extension; contribution rises if feet are placed further from the hips.
Posterior (ischiocondylar) fibers act as a powerful hip extensor, especially in the deeper hip-flexed portion of the range.

Work largely isometrically to hold the vertical shin and control knee position (knee angle stays roughly constant, so they stabilize rather than drive the movement); more involved with feet closer to the hips.
Controls frontal-plane pelvis position and resists knee valgus/hip drop.
Maintain a neutral trunk on the bench; should not substitute lumbar extension for hip extension.
With the obliques, maintains rib-down position and the posterior pelvic tilt at lockout.
Assists in holding posterior pelvic tilt and preventing rib flare/lumbar hyperextension.
Hip flexor lengthened at terminal hip extension at the top of the rep.
Biarticular hip flexor lengthened across the hip at lockout while the knee remains flexed.

cues.text: Drive the floor away through your heels.
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top and tuck your pelvis slightly under.
Finish with shins vertical and your body flat like a tabletop from knees to shoulders.
Keep your chin tucked and ribs down — look toward your knees, not the ceiling.

regressions.name: Glute Bridge
regressions.description: Perform the same hip-extension pattern from the floor with a shorter range of motion and no bench, reducing load and setup demand.

regressions.name: Bodyweight Hip Thrust with Pause
regressions.description: Perform the bench-supported hip thrust without external load, holding 2-3 seconds at the top to groove terminal hip extension and glute activation.

progressions.name: Barbell Hip Thrust
progressions.description: Add a padded barbell across the hip crease and progressively load; allows heavy progressive overload of hip extension.

progressions.name: Single-Leg Hip Thrust
progressions.description: Perform on one leg (or B-stance as an intermediate step) to increase load per limb and add frontal-plane pelvic stability demand.

dosing: For strength/hypertrophy: 3-4 sets of 6-12 reps, 2-3 times per week, with progressive loading — consistent with general resistance-training guidelines; no hip-thrust-specific dosing standard exists.
emgNotes: Surface EMG studies (notably work by Contreras and colleagues) consistently show very high gluteus maximus activation during the barbell hip thrust, with peak activity at terminal hip extension — often exceeding that measured in the back squat at comparable relative loads, since the horizontal loading vector keeps the hip-extension moment high at lockout. Biceps femoris and vastus lateralis show moderate activity, with hamstring contribution increasing as feet move further from the hips and quadriceps contribution increasing as feet move closer. Longitudinal training studies suggest the hip thrust and squat produce broadly comparable gluteal hypertrophy, so its main distinction is the activation profile and end-range emphasis rather than proven superior growth.
unmappedWanted: gluteus maximus upper vs lower fiber subdivision (roster has only whole gluteus-maximus)

name: Romanian Deadlift
description: Stand holding a barbell or dumbbells in front of the thighs with a soft (slightly bent, fixed) knee angle. Hinge at the hips by pushing them backward while keeping the spine neutral and the load close to the legs, lowering until you feel a firm hamstring stretch (typically mid-shin to just below the knee), then drive the hips forward to return to standing. The knee angle stays nearly constant throughout — the movement occurs at the hip, not the knee.

muscleRoles.notes: Biarticular hamstring loaded eccentrically at long length on the descent and extending the hip on the ascent; primary target.
Medial hamstring controlling the hinge descent and extending the hip on the ascent.
Medial hamstring contributing hip-extension torque throughout the movement.
Major hip extensor, especially over the top half of the lift into terminal extension at lockout.
Ischial (hamstring-part) fibers act as a powerful hip extensor, especially in the deeper hinge positions.
Contracts largely isometrically to hold the spine neutral against the flexion moment of the load.
Segmental lumbar stabilization while resisting spinal flexion under load.
Assists frontal- and sagittal-plane trunk stiffness during the hinge.
Contributes to intra-abdominal pressure and trunk bracing.
Keeps the bar pulled close to the body and adds trunk stiffness via the thoracolumbar fascia.
Maintains scapular position against the downward pull of the load.
Scapular retraction and postural control of the upper back.
Grip: holds the implement isometrically. Reclassified from synergist — grip stabilizes the hold, it does not assist hip extension.
Grip: sustained isometric finger flexion on the bar. Reclassified from synergist for the same reason.
Assists posterior knee/tibial stability and balance as the hips travel backward.

cues.text: Push your hips back toward the wall behind you, like closing a car door with your backside.
Drag the bar down your thighs — keep it touching or nearly touching your legs the whole way.
Keep a proud chest and long spine; stop the descent when your hamstrings feel loaded, not when your back starts to round.
Soft knees that don't move — bend from the hips, not the knees; this is a hinge, not a squat.

regressions.name: Dowel hip hinge
regressions.description: Practice the hinge pattern unloaded with a dowel held along the spine (contacting head, mid-back, and sacrum) to groove neutral-spine hip flexion before adding load.

regressions.name: Kettlebell RDL to elevated target
regressions.description: Perform the hinge with a light kettlebell, lowering only to a box or bench that shortens the range of motion, reducing the flexibility and control demand.

progressions.name: Single-leg Romanian deadlift
progressions.description: Perform the hinge on one leg, adding balance, frontal-plane pelvic control, and unilateral hamstring/glute demand; regress from the roster's single-leg hip hinge with bench support if needed.

progressions.name: Tempo or deficit barbell RDL
progressions.description: Increase eccentric time (3-5 s lowering) or stand on a low platform to extend range of motion, increasing time under tension at long hamstring lengths.

dosing: Strength/hypertrophy: 3-4 sets of 6-10 reps at a controlled tempo, 1-2 sessions per week. For hamstring injury-risk reduction, eccentric-biased hinge work at long muscle lengths is commonly programmed 1-2x/week alongside (not replacing) high-eccentric exercises such as the Nordic hamstring curl.
emgNotes: Comparative surface-EMG studies of hamstring exercises consistently show the Romanian deadlift produces high activation of the biceps femoris long head and semitendinosus, in the same tier as the good morning and glute-ham raise, with hamstring activity typically greatest during the eccentric-to-concentric transition near peak hip flexion. Relative to knee-flexion-dominant exercises (leg curl, Nordic curl), the RDL biases hip-extension-based hamstring loading and tends to emphasize the biceps femoris long head somewhat more than the semitendinosus. Erector spinae activity is substantial but predominantly isometric when a neutral spine is maintained. Gluteus maximus activation rises toward lockout as the hip approaches full extension.
