trunk anti-flexion / spinal bracing (no anti-movement category in roster movements)
forearm flexor group / grip (mapped via individual finger flexors instead)

name: Seated Calf Raise
description: Sit upright on a bench or seated calf-raise machine with the knees bent to roughly 90 degrees and the balls of both feet on a raised platform or foot plate, heels hanging free. Rest the resistance (machine pad, dumbbell, or barbell) across the lower thighs just above the knees. Let the heels drop below the platform to feel a stretch through the back of the lower leg, then press through the balls of the feet to raise the heels as high as possible into full plantarflexion. Pause briefly at the top, then lower under control back to the stretched position. Because the knee is held in flexion, the gastrocnemius is shortened and slackened, shifting the workload onto the soleus.

muscleRoles.notes: Chief plantarflexor in this exercise; knee flexion slackens the gastrocnemius, so the soleus becomes the dominant mover. Predominantly slow-twitch (type I), so it tolerates and responds to higher-rep loading.

Contributes to plantarflexion but is placed at a mechanical/length (active-insufficiency) disadvantage by the flexed knee, so its involvement is substantially reduced compared with a standing straight-knee calf raise.
Weak accessory plantarflexor; assists the raise and supports the medial arch and subtalar stability.
Accessory plantarflexor; assists push-off through the great toe at the top of the range.
Accessory plantarflexor; assists and helps grip the platform through the lesser toes.
Accessory plantarflexor that also provides lateral ankle stability during the movement.
Accessory plantarflexor that stabilizes the lateral ankle.
Negligible accessory plantarflexor; contribution is small and further reduced by knee flexion since it also crosses the knee.
Primary dorsiflexor; lengthens and is placed on stretch as the heels rise into plantarflexion.
Dorsiflexor/toe extensor that lengthens as the ankle plantarflexes.

cues.text: Drive the balls of your feet down into the platform and push the floor away to lift your heels as high as they will go.
Let the heels sink well below the platform at the bottom for a full stretch, then reach the tallest possible heel position at the top through the full range.
Pause and squeeze for a beat at the top, then lower slowly and under control rather than dropping the weight.
Keep the knees bent to about 90 degrees and the load stacked over the ankles so the effort stays in the calf, not the thigh.

regressions.name: Bodyweight seated raise
regressions.description: Perform the movement with no added load (or only the empty machine carriage), using just the weight of the legs to groove the pattern and build initial tolerance.

regressions.name: Reduced range of motion
regressions.description: Limit the depth of the heel drop and work through a shorter, more comfortable arc before progressing to a full stretched-to-shortened range.

progressions.name: Loaded / heavier seated raise
progressions.description: Progressively add weight (machine stack, dumbbells, or barbell across the thighs) while maintaining full range and a controlled tempo.

progressions.name: Single-leg seated calf raise
progressions.description: Perform one leg at a time to roughly double the relative load and expose side-to-side strength differences.

emgNotes: The defining feature of the seated calf raise is knee flexion. The gastrocnemius crosses both the knee and the ankle, so flexing the knee to ~90 degrees shortens and slackens it, reducing its ability to generate force. As a result, surface EMG and biomechanical work consistently show the soleus as the dominant plantarflexor in the knee-flexed (seated) position, whereas the gastrocnemius contributes proportionally more during standing, straight-knee calf raises. The soleus is largely composed of slow-twitch (type I) fibers, which is the common rationale for training it with higher repetitions and longer time under tension. Exact activation percentages vary by study and setup and are not cited here.
dosing: General resistance-training guidance applies: roughly 2-3 sessions per week, about 2-4 sets of 10-20 repetitions with a controlled tempo and full range, progressing load over time. Higher rep ranges are commonly used for the soleus given its slow-twitch composition. Calf-specific dosing evidence is limited, so these are practical, expert-consensus targets rather than exercise-specific trial findings.

name: Sit-Up
description: Lie supine with knees bent to about 90 degrees and feet flat on the floor, arms crossed over the chest or hands lightly behind the ears. Curl the trunk by flexing the spine segmentally, then continue rising through hip flexion until the torso is upright, and lower back down with control to the start position.

muscleRoles.notes: Drives the initial trunk-curl phase (thoracic and lumbar flexion), roughly the first 30-45 degrees of the movement.
Dominant in the second phase once the trunk clears the floor; a full sit-up is as much a hip-flexion exercise as a trunk-flexion exercise, distinguishing it from a crunch.
Assists trunk flexion and controls trunk alignment; activity rises if any rotation is added.
Assists trunk flexion and contributes to intra-abdominal pressure.
Assists hip flexion in the sit-to-upright phase, especially when the feet are anchored.
Flexes the cervical spine to lift the head at initiation; over-recruitment indicates poor technique.
Provides anticipatory abdominal bracing and intra-abdominal pressure.
Minor hip-flexion assist during the second phase.

Antagonist that lengthens as the spine flexes on the concentric rise. Note: the lowering (return) phase is trunk extension controlled eccentrically by the flexors resisting gravity, so erector spinae stays relatively quiet rather than driving the descent.

cues.text: Peel your spine off the floor one segment at a time rather than jerking up in one piece.
Imagine curling your ribs down toward your pelvis as you rise.
Keep your chin gently tucked, as if holding a small orange under it, so your neck does not lead the movement.
Lower down as slowly as you came up, letting the floor meet your back one segment at a time.

regressions.name: Crunch (partial curl-up)
regressions.description: Curl only the head and shoulder blades off the floor, keeping the low back down. Reduces range, hip-flexor demand, and lumbar load while still training the trunk flexors.

regressions.name: McGill curl-up
regressions.description: With hands under the lumbar spine and one knee bent, lift only the head and shoulders a few centimeters while maintaining a neutral spine. Trains trunk-flexor endurance with minimal spinal flexion, useful for those with low-back sensitivity.

progressions.name: Decline sit-up
progressions.description: Perform on a decline bench with the head below the hips, increasing the gravitational moment on the trunk and hip flexors through a longer range.

progressions.name: Weighted sit-up
progressions.description: Hold a plate or dumbbell at the chest (or overhead for greater challenge) to add external load and allow progressive overload in lower rep ranges.

emgNotes: EMG studies consistently show the sit-up is a two-phase movement: rectus abdominis and the obliques dominate the initial trunk-curl (roughly the first third of range), after which the hip flexors — psoas major, iliacus, and rectus femoris — become the primary torque producers as the trunk rises off the floor. Indwelling-EMG work has demonstrated substantial psoas activation during both bent-knee and straight-leg sit-ups, with bent knees not eliminating hip-flexor involvement as once believed. Anchoring the feet further increases hip-flexor and rectus femoris recruitment. Spine biomechanics research (notably McGill's laboratory) has measured lumbar compression during repeated full sit-ups at levels approaching occupational action limits (on the order of 3000+ N), which is the basis for preferring curl-up variations in low-back pain populations. Trunk-flexor activation in a partial curl-up is comparable to the full sit-up with markedly lower lumbar load, which is why the crunch and McGill curl-up serve as regressions rather than fundamentally different exercises.
unmappedWanted: psoas-major (only combined iliopsoas available in roster)
iliacus (only combined iliopsoas available in roster)

rectus-capitis / longus-colli deep neck flexors as a distinct cervical-flexion synergist (roster deep-cervical-flexors could arguably apply but was omitted as their role in a standard sit-up is minor)

name: Crunch
description: Lie supine with knees bent about 90 degrees and feet flat on the floor, hands lightly supporting the head or crossed over the chest. Exhale and curl the trunk, lifting the head and shoulder blades off the floor by flexing the spine — drawing the ribs toward the pelvis — while the low back stays in contact with the floor. Lower back down with control; unlike a full sit-up, the movement stops well short of sitting upright, which limits hip flexor involvement.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime trunk flexor; drives the rib-cage-toward-pelvis curl throughout the range.
Assists trunk flexion bilaterally; activity rises in twisting/oblique crunch variants.
Assists trunk flexion and contributes to the posterior pelvic tilt bilaterally.
Deep abdominal wall tension and intra-abdominal pressure regulation; produces no flexion torque.
Flexes the cervical spine to carry the head with the trunk; cue against over-recruiting it to avoid neck strain.
Spinal extensors lengthen as the thoracolumbar spine flexes — the stretched antagonist.

Reclassified from synergist. In a short-range crunch the hip angle does not change and the pelvis moves toward posterior tilt, so iliopsoas does not synergize spinal flexion; it provides only low-level pelvic/hip anchoring and is deliberately minimized versus a sit-up by keeping the range short.

cues.text: Draw your ribs down toward your pelvis as you curl up.
Peel your shoulder blades off the floor, then lower back down one segment at a time.
Keep a fist-sized gap between your chin and chest — don't pull on your head with your hands.
Exhale as you curl up, inhale as you lower.

regressions.name: Posterior pelvic tilt
regressions.description: Supine with knees bent, flatten the low back into the floor by tilting the pelvis and gently drawing ribs down, without lifting the head or shoulders. Builds the initiating abdominal pattern with minimal load.

regressions.name: Partial-range head-and-shoulder lift
regressions.description: Perform the crunch through a smaller range — lifting just the head and top of the shoulder blades — with hands fully supporting the head to offload the neck.

progressions.name: Weighted crunch
progressions.description: Hold a plate or dumbbell at the chest (or extended overhead for a longer lever) and perform the same curl-up, progressing load gradually.

progressions.name: Decline or stability-ball crunch
progressions.description: Perform the crunch on a decline bench to increase resistance, or on a stability ball to add range of motion (pre-stretch over the ball) and a balance demand.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 10-20 controlled repetitions, 2-3 sessions per week, consistent with general resistance-training guidelines for trunk musculature; progress by adding load or lever length rather than high-repetition volume.
emgNotes: Surface EMG studies consistently show the crunch produces high rectus abdominis activation and moderate external and internal oblique activation, and it compares favorably with — or exceeds — many commercial abdominal devices. Compared with a full sit-up, the shortened range keeps hip flexor (iliopsoas/rectus femoris) activity substantially lower, which is why the crunch is preferred when lumbar shear from hip flexor pull is a concern. Claims of selectively isolating the "upper" versus "lower" rectus abdominis with crunch variants are not well supported; regional differences reported across studies are small and inconsistent. Hands-behind-head performance tends to increase sternocleidomastoid recruitment if the head is pulled, which is a technique fault rather than a training target.

name: Russian Twist
description: Sit on the floor with knees bent and heels resting lightly on the ground, then lean the trunk back about 45 degrees while keeping the spine long and chest lifted. Clasp the hands (or hold a weight) in front of the chest and rotate the ribcage to bring the hands toward the floor beside one hip, then rotate under control to the opposite side. Maintain the reclined trunk angle and abdominal brace throughout; the rotation should come from the trunk, not just the arms swinging.

muscleRoles.notes: Drives contralateral trunk rotation; the external oblique opposite the direction of turn is the dominant rotator.
Ipsilateral rotation partner of the opposite external oblique; the pair alternates side to side each rep.
Isometrically maintains the reclined trunk angle against gravity and contributes trunk-flexion torque.
Deep segmental rotators assisting and controlling thoracic rotation.
Segmental control of the lumbar spine during rotation.
Provides the abdominal brace that stiffens the trunk cylinder throughout the hold.
Prevents the reclined spine from collapsing into full flexion; keeps the trunk long.
Frontal-plane and rotary stabilization of the lumbar spine and pelvis.
Isometric hip flexion holds the reclined V-sit position, especially when feet are lifted.
Assists isometric hip flexion in the reclined/V-sit hold.

cues.text: Sit tall, then hinge back until you feel your abs switch on — keep your chest proud rather than slumping.
Move the ball (or your hands) in a wide arc from hip to hip — let your ribcage turn to carry it there.
Rotate from your trunk, not your arms — your breastbone should point toward each side as you turn.
Exhale as you rotate to each side; keep a steady brace instead of holding your breath.

regressions.name: Feet-Down Russian Twist
regressions.description: Keep both heels on the floor and use hands only (no weight), reducing the hip-flexor and trunk-flexion demand so the focus stays on controlled rotation.

regressions.name: Seated Upright Trunk Rotation
regressions.description: Sit fully upright on a chair or the floor without the reclined hold and rotate the trunk side to side, removing the anti-gravity flexion component entirely.

progressions.name: Feet-Elevated Russian Twist
progressions.description: Lift the feet off the floor into a full V-sit while rotating, substantially increasing hip-flexor and anterior-core demand.

progressions.name: Weighted Russian Twist
progressions.description: Hold a medicine ball, dumbbell, or plate and touch it to the floor beside each hip, adding rotary load; progress weight gradually while keeping the spine long.

emgNotes: Surface EMG work on trunk-rotation exercises shows high activation of the external and internal obliques, with the obliques working as contralateral pairs (the external oblique opposite the turn with the internal oblique on the side of the turn). Rectus abdominis activity is moderate and largely isometric, sustaining the reclined trunk position, and it rises further when the feet are lifted. Direct EMG data on the Russian twist itself is sparse; most inferences come from studies of related rotational and curl-up-type exercises. Clinically, the combination of sustained lumbar flexion with repeated rotation under load raises disc-loading concerns in spine-biomechanics literature, so it is generally avoided or heavily modified for patients with flexion- or rotation-sensitive low back pain.
dosing: No exercise-specific published dosing exists. General resistance-training guidance suggests 2-3 sets of 8-15 controlled rotations per side, 2-3 times weekly, progressing load or lever length once form is stable.

name: Mountain Climber
description: Begin in a high plank with hands under shoulders, body in a straight line from head to heels. Drive one knee toward the chest, then quickly switch legs in a running-like motion while keeping the trunk rigid and hips level. Continue alternating at a controlled or brisk tempo without letting the hips pike up or sag.
bodyPosition: high plank (prone support on hands and toes)

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover of the rapid hip flexion driving each knee toward the chest.
Works isometrically to resist lumbar extension and keep the trunk rigid against the alternating leg drive; a signature target of the exercise.
Biarticular assistant to iliopsoas in hip flexion during the knee drive (also contributes to knee re-extension); downgraded from primary since iliopsoas is the true prime mover.
Resists trunk rotation and controls pelvic position as the legs alternate.
Works with external oblique for anti-rotation and pelvic stabilization.
Vasti stabilize/extend the stance-leg knee and help re-extend the driving leg.
Assists hip flexion during the knee drive.
Provides deep trunk stiffness throughout the movement.
Keeps the pelvis level in the frontal plane during single-leg support phases.
Maintains scapular protraction and stability on the ribcage in the plank position.
Isometrically supports the shoulder in the weight-bearing plank position.
Assists shoulder stabilization while bearing weight through the arms.
Holds the elbows extended isometrically throughout.
Co-contracts with the abdominals to keep the spine neutral.
Provides segmental lumbar stability against the alternating limb load.
Lengthens on the flexing hip with each knee drive while working isometrically to hold the opposite extended hip in line.

cues.text: Push the floor away so your shoulders stay stacked directly over your wrists.
Imagine balancing a tray of water on your low back — keep hips level, no piking or sagging.
Drive the knee toward your chest without letting your back round or your hips hike.
Keep your breathing steady and rhythmic; don't hold your breath as the pace increases.

regressions.name: Incline Mountain Climber
regressions.description: Perform with hands elevated on a bench, box, or countertop to reduce load on the shoulders and core, making it easier to keep the trunk rigid.

regressions.name: Slow March Mountain Climber
regressions.description: From a high plank, slowly step one knee in, pause, and return before switching legs — removes the ballistic component and emphasizes controlled trunk stability.

progressions.name: Cross-Body Mountain Climber
progressions.description: Drive each knee toward the opposite elbow, adding a rotational demand that increases oblique challenge.

progressions.name: Slider Mountain Climber
progressions.description: Perform with feet on sliders or towels so the legs glide rather than hop, requiring continuous tension and greater hip flexor and core control; can also be progressed by increasing tempo to a sprint pace.

emgNotes: Surface EMG work on plank-based dynamic core exercises shows the mountain climber elicits moderate-to-high activation of the rectus abdominis and obliques, generally exceeding a static front plank because the alternating leg drive adds anti-rotation and anti-extension perturbation. Cross-body variations tend to shift relative activation toward the obliques. Hip flexor (iliopsoas, rectus femoris) demand is high on the driving leg, and serratus anterior plus anterior deltoid are active throughout to stabilize the weight-bearing shoulder. Published EMG data specific to this exercise are limited compared with squat or deadlift literature, so these patterns are drawn from small comparative studies of plank variants and from biomechanical reasoning.
unmappedWanted: pectoralis-minor (scapular stabilizer in plank support)
psoas-major (only combined iliopsoas available in roster)

name: Burpee
description: From standing, squat down and place both hands on the floor just inside the feet, jump or step the feet back into a rigid high plank, perform an optional push-up, then jump the feet back toward the hands and explode vertically into a jump with arms reaching overhead. Land softly with knees tracking over the toes and flow immediately into the next repetition.
bodyPosition: standing, transitioning through squat and prone plank each repetition

muscleRoles.notes: Concentric knee extension driving the squat-up and vertical jump; eccentric control on the squat-down and landing.
Hip extension power for the jump and rise from the squat; controls hip flexion on descent.
Ankle plantarflexion in the jump take-off and the hop back to plank.
Horizontal adduction during the OPTIONAL push-up phase only; not a prime mover of the burpee, so downgraded from primary.
Elbow extension in the optional push-up and isometric/eccentric control catching body weight at the hand plant; not a prime concentric driver of the exercise, so downgraded from primary.
Assist hip extension in the jump; lengthen under load during the standing fold to place hands on the floor.
Plantarflexion during take-off and eccentric control of dorsiflexion on landing.
Shoulder flexion in the optional push-up and, non-optionally, arm drive overhead in the jump.
Rapid hip flexion snapping the feet from plank back under the trunk.
Combined hip flexion and knee extension demand in the jump-in and take-off.
Scapular protraction/upward rotation keeping the scapula fixed to the thorax during plank and push-up; this is scapulothoracic stabilization, reclassified from synergist.
Prevents lumbar extension sag in plank and resists trunk extension moments during the jump-back.
Anterolateral trunk bracing through plank transitions.
Anticipatory trunk stiffness during rapid position changes.
Maintains neutral spine in plank and during rapid trunk repositioning; lengthens during the forward fold.
Frontal-plane pelvic control on take-off and landing.

cues.text: Hands land directly under your shoulders — brace your trunk before your feet leave the ground.
Kick back to one rigid plank line from head to heels — no sagging hips or piked bottom.
On the jump, push the floor away and reach for the ceiling.
Land softly through the whole foot with knees tracking over your toes, then flow straight into the next rep.

regressions.name: Step-back burpee (no push-up, no jump)
regressions.description: Step one foot back at a time into plank, step back in, and finish with a stand or heel raise instead of a jump. Removes the impact and plyometric demand while keeping the whole-body transition pattern.

regressions.name: Elevated-surface burpee
regressions.description: Perform the movement with hands on a bench, box, or countertop. The incline reduces upper-limb loading and the depth of hip and ankle flexion required.

progressions.name: Burpee with push-up and tuck jump
progressions.description: Add a full chest-to-floor push-up in the plank phase and finish with a tuck jump, increasing upper-body loading and plyometric intensity.

progressions.name: Burpee box jump-over
progressions.description: Finish each repetition by jumping onto or laterally over a box, adding a higher-amplitude jump and landing demand plus a directional change.

dosing: No burpee-specific dosing guideline exists; in published interval-training studies burpees are typically programmed as work-rest intervals (e.g., 20-30 s maximal effort with equal or double rest for 4-8 rounds, 2-3 sessions/week) or as 3 sets of 8-15 repetitions for general conditioning, adjusted to maintain landing mechanics.
emgNotes: Formal EMG data specific to the burpee are sparse; muscle roles here are inferred from the constituent phases, each of which is well characterized: the squat and jump phases demand high quadriceps, gluteus maximus, and triceps surae activation, the plank/push-up phase loads pectoralis major, triceps brachii, anterior deltoid, and serratus anterior, and the abdominal wall and spinal extensors work isometrically to control the trunk through rapid transitions. Physiological studies of burpee interval protocols consistently show near-maximal heart rate and high oxygen-uptake responses, supporting its use as a whole-body cardiometabolic conditioning exercise more than as a targeted strengthening exercise.
unmappedWanted: triple extension / vertical jump (composite movement pattern)
trunk anti-extension (plank bracing) as a movement category
cardiorespiratory conditioning attribute

name: Jumping Jack
description: Stand tall with feet together and arms at your sides. Jump both feet out wider than hip-width while swinging straight arms out and overhead, then immediately jump back to the start position with arms returning to your sides. Repeat rhythmically, landing softly on the balls of the feet each cycle.

muscleRoles.notes: Drives the hip abduction phase as the legs jump apart and controls frontal-plane pelvis position on landing.
Main mover for the arm swing into abduction toward overhead.
Concentric plantarflexion powers each takeoff; works eccentrically to absorb landing.
Shares plantarflexion propulsion and cushions repeated low-amplitude landings.
Pulls the legs back to midline on the return jump (a lower-demand, partly momentum-assisted phase) and works eccentrically to decelerate the out-phase abduction.
Assists hip abduction and pelvic stability.
Assists hip abduction during the out-jump.
Assists as the arms travel through the higher arc toward overhead.
Initiates and assists glenohumeral abduction each rep.
Contributes to scapular upward rotation as the arms elevate overhead.
Force couple with upper trapezius and serratus anterior for scapular upward rotation.
Upwardly rotates the scapula so the arms can reach overhead.
Powers the arm return into adduction/extension on the down-swing.
Small knee extension for takeoff and shock absorption on each landing.
Keeps the trunk upright and resists rib flare as the arms go overhead.
Anterolateral trunk control during rapid limb movement.
Deep trunk stiffness for repeated impact cycles.
Maintains upright spinal posture against the overhead arm swing.
Controls the foot/ankle in flight and at landing and helps reposition the foot each cycle.
Lateral ankle stability during widened-stance landings.
Sternal fibers are lengthened as the antagonist at the top of the overhead arm position.

cues.text: Land softly on the balls of your feet, letting the knees and ankles give a little on each landing.
Swing the arms through a full arc — hands touch (or nearly touch) overhead, then return to your sides.
Keep your ribs stacked over your pelvis — don't arch the low back as the arms go overhead.
Find a steady, springy rhythm; jump only as high as needed to spread and close the feet.

regressions.name: Step Jack (Low-Impact Jack)
regressions.description: Alternate stepping one foot out to the side while performing the same overhead arm swing, eliminating the jump and impact. Useful for deconditioned patients, joint pain, or early return to activity.

regressions.name: Arms-Only Jack
regressions.description: Stand (or sit) with feet still and perform only the rhythmic arm abduction-adduction swing. Trains the shoulder pattern and raises heart rate without lower-extremity impact.

progressions.name: Power Jack (Squat Jack)
progressions.description: Jump the feet out into a quarter-squat landing and explode back to standing, increasing lower-extremity loading and plyometric demand.

progressions.name: Weighted or Extended-Duration Jacks
progressions.description: Add light dumbbells or a resistance band at the arms, or extend continuous work intervals (e.g., 45-60 seconds at pace) to increase strength-endurance and cardiovascular demand.

emgNotes: Published EMG data specific to jumping jacks are sparse; activation is inferred from the component motions. The out-phase demands rapid concentric hip abduction (gluteus medius/minimus, TFL) with the adductor group decelerating eccentrically, and the pattern reverses on the return, making it a light reciprocal frontal-plane conditioning drill for the hip. The calf complex (gastrocnemius/soleus) cycles between concentric propulsion and eccentric landing absorption at low amplitude, similar to submaximal hopping. The overhead arm swing recruits the deltoid with the scapular upward-rotation force couple (trapezius and serratus anterior) at moderate, rhythmic intensities. Trunk musculature works isometrically to maintain upright posture against the fast bilateral limb movement. Overall intensity per muscle is low to moderate — the exercise functions primarily as aerobic conditioning, warm-up, and low-level plyometric coordination rather than a strengthening stimulus.

name: Jump Squat
description: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, descend quickly into a quarter-to-half squat with arms swinging back, then explosively extend the hips, knees, and ankles to jump vertically as high as possible. Land softly toes-to-heels into the same squat depth, absorbing force with bent hips and knees, then reset or immediately rebound depending on the training goal.
equipment: optional: dumbbells or trap bar for loaded variations

muscleRoles.notes: Concentric knee extension is a prime driver of vertical propulsion; high eccentric demand controlling knee flexion on landing.
Explosive hip extension is the dominant force generator at takeoff; eccentric hip control during landing absorption.
Fast, biarticular plantarflexor central to the stretch-shortening cycle and the final phase of triple extension at takeoff.
Contributes plantarflexion force and shock absorption, but as the slow/postural plantarflexor it is a supporting contributor to explosive push-off rather than a prime mover — downgraded from primary.
Assist hip extension and provide biarticular energy transfer between hip and knee during propulsion.
Ischiocondylar (posterior) fibers assist hip extension, especially from deeper countermovement positions.
Maintains neutral trunk against the flexion moment during countermovement and landing.
Frontal-plane pelvic and knee control; resists dynamic knee valgus on takeoff and landing.
Anterior trunk stiffness for force transfer through the core.
Deep trunk bracing during rapid loading.
Pre-activates before ground contact to position the ankle for a stable dorsiflexed landing.
Lateral ankle stability and eversion control during landing.
Hip flexors are stretched as the antagonist at full hip extension during takeoff.

cues.text: Push the floor away and jump toward the ceiling as fast as you can
Land as quietly as possible — soft like landing on eggshells
Keep your knees tracking over your toes on takeoff and landing — no caving in
Swing your arms back on the dip, then throw them up as you jump

regressions.name: Squat to Heel Raise
regressions.description: Perform a bodyweight squat and rise onto the toes at the top without leaving the ground; grooves the triple-extension pattern with no flight or landing impact.

regressions.name: Box Jump
regressions.description: Jump onto an elevated box so the landing occurs near the jump apex, dramatically reducing eccentric landing forces while keeping the explosive takeoff.

progressions.name: Loaded Jump Squat
progressions.description: Add external load (dumbbells, trap bar, or barbell at roughly 0-30% of squat 1RM) to increase power demands while preserving movement velocity.

progressions.name: Depth Jump
progressions.description: Step off a 20-40 cm box and immediately rebound into a maximal vertical jump on ground contact, increasing stretch-shortening-cycle intensity and reactive strength demand.

dosing: Consistent with plyometric training guidelines: 2-3 sessions per week on non-consecutive days, 3-5 sets of 3-8 maximal-effort reps, with full recovery (60-180 s) between sets to preserve jump quality; keep total session volume modest (roughly 40-100 ground contacts) for lower-extremity plyometrics. Loaded variations for peak power development are typically performed at 0-30% of squat 1RM.
emgNotes: Surface EMG and biomechanics studies of countermovement and jump squats show high activation of the quadriceps (vastus lateralis and medialis) and gluteus maximus during the propulsion phase, with the gastrocnemius and soleus contributing strongly in the final portion of triple extension. The hamstrings act biarticularly, assisting hip extension while transferring energy across the knee. Landing imposes large eccentric demands on the quadriceps and plantarflexors, and the tibialis anterior pre-activates prior to ground contact. Trunk musculature (erector spinae, abdominals) provides stiffness for force transfer, and gluteus medius activity is important for frontal-plane knee control, a common focus in ACL injury-prevention programs.

name: Wall Sit
description: Stand with your back flat against a wall, feet hip-width apart and about 1.5-2 foot-lengths away from the wall. Slide down until your hips and knees are bent to roughly 90 degrees with shins vertical, and hold the position isometrically while breathing normally. Slide back up the wall to finish the set.

muscleRoles.notes: Isometric knee extension against gravity is the principal demand; activation rises as knee flexion approaches 90 degrees.
Isometric hip extension holds the ~90-degree hip against further flexion; demand is modest given the wall-supported vertical torso.
Biarticular co-contraction across hip and knee; contributes to joint stiffness and assists hip extension rather than producing primary torque.
Frontal-plane pelvic and knee alignment; prevents knee valgus drift during the hold.
Frontal-plane thigh control; activation increases if a ball squeeze is added between the knees.
Maintains neutral trunk position against the wall (low-level given wall support).
Low-level trunk bracing supports the flat-back position.
Controls the tibia over the near-neutral/dorsiflexed ankle with vertical shins.
Low-level antagonist co-contraction maintaining ankle position and balance through the foot.

cues.text: Press your whole back — low back included — flat into the wall.
Push the floor away through your heels, as if trying to slide the wall down.
Keep shins vertical: knees stacked over ankles, not drifting past the toes.
Breathe steadily throughout the hold — do not hold your breath or shrug your shoulders.

regressions.name: High Wall Sit (partial depth)
regressions.description: Slide only partway down the wall (about 30-45 degrees of knee flexion) to reduce quadriceps demand and patellofemoral load; useful early in knee rehab.

regressions.name: Short-Duration Intervals
regressions.description: Perform brief 10-15 second holds with generous rest, accumulating time across multiple sets rather than one long hold.

progressions.name: Weighted Wall Sit
progressions.description: Hold a dumbbell, kettlebell, or plate on the thighs (or wear a loaded backpack) while maintaining the 90/90 position.

progressions.name: Single-Leg Wall Sit
progressions.description: From the standard hold, extend one knee to lift that foot off the floor, doubling the load on the stance limb; alternate sides.

dosing: For local muscular endurance: 3-5 holds of 20-60 seconds, 2-3 sessions per week, progressing hold time or load. For blood-pressure reduction, isometric exercise training trials commonly used 4 x 2-minute wall-squat holds with about 2 minutes rest, 3 sessions per week, at an individually calibrated knee angle or perceived effort.
emgNotes: Surface EMG studies of isometric wall squats consistently show the quadriceps — particularly vastus lateralis and vastus medialis — as the dominant muscles, with activation increasing as knee flexion deepens toward 90 degrees; rectus femoris contributes less than the vasti because its hip-flexor moment is unloaded by the wall support. Hamstring co-activation is low, which is one reason the wall sit is favored when tibiofemoral shear must be limited. Gluteal and trunk-extensor activity is modest and postural. Adding an isometric ball squeeze between the knees measurably raises adductor and vastus medialis activity. Separately, isometric wall-squat training has a substantial randomized-trial literature (including meta-analyses of isometric exercise training) showing clinically meaningful reductions in resting blood pressure.

name: Superman
description: Lie prone with arms extended overhead and legs straight. Simultaneously lift both arms, chest, and both legs a few centimeters off the floor by extending the spine and hips, keeping the neck neutral (gaze at the floor), hold briefly, then lower with control.

muscleRoles.notes: Main driver of trunk extension against gravity; longissimus and iliocostalis work along the full thoracolumbar spine.
Extends the hips to lift the legs; cueing a glute squeeze before lifting reduces reliance on lumbar hyperextension.
Segmental extensor and stabilizer of the lumbar spine during the hold.
Assist hip extension while the knees stay straight.
Raises the overhead arms off the floor against gravity in the prone position, working with the scapular muscles.
Upwardly rotates and posteriorly tilts the scapula to stabilize it for the overhead arm position (does not itself lift the arm).
Assists scapular retraction/control as the arms lift.
Contributes to thoracic extension.
Gentle abdominal brace protects the lumbar spine and limits excessive extension.
Frontal-plane stabilizer of the lumbar spine and pelvis during the bilateral lift.
Anterior trunk is lengthened as the spine extends.
Hip flexors are stretched as the hips extend from the prone position.

cues.text: Squeeze your glutes first, then lift arms and legs together.
Reach long through your fingertips and toes, as if being pulled from both ends.
Keep your nose pointed at the floor so your neck stays in line with your spine.
Think 'long and low' — a few centimeters of lift, not maximum height.

regressions.name: Bird Dog
regressions.description: Quadruped opposite arm and leg raise trains the same posterior chain with far lower lumbar compressive load; the preferred entry point for back-pain patients.

regressions.name: Prone Alternating Arm/Leg Raise
regressions.description: From prone, lift only one arm and the opposite leg at a time, reducing the extension moment and total load on the lumbar spine.

progressions.name: Superman with Long Hold or Pulses
progressions.description: Extend the isometric hold to 10-20 seconds or add small alternating arm/leg pulses ('swimmers') to increase time under tension and endurance demand.

progressions.name: Weighted or Ball Superman
progressions.description: Hold light dumbbells or a light plate in the outstretched hands, or perform the extension over a stability ball to increase range and load.

emgNotes: Surface EMG studies of prone trunk-extension exercises consistently show moderate-to-high activation of the lumbar and thoracic erector spinae and multifidus, with concurrent gluteus maximus and hamstring activity when the legs are lifted. Biomechanical modeling work (notably Stuart McGill's spine-loading research) has estimated that the full bilateral superman imposes among the highest lumbar compressive loads of common trunk-extensor exercises — on the order of several thousand newtons — which is why bird dog is generally preferred early in low-back rehabilitation and superman is reserved for asymptomatic or later-stage patients. Lifting the arms overhead in prone also produces meaningful lower trapezius and posterior deltoid activity, similar to a prone Y raise.

name: Bear Crawl
description: Start in quadruped with hands under shoulders, toes tucked, and knees lifted about 2-5 cm off the floor. Crawl forward by moving the opposite hand and foot together in small steps while keeping the spine neutral, hips level with the shoulders, and knees hovering low. Move slowly and with control, then reverse or continue for the prescribed distance or time.
bodyPosition: quadruped (knees hovering, hands and toes weight-bearing)

muscleRoles.notes: Maintains scapular protraction and scapulothoracic control during hand weight-bearing and reaching steps
Drives shoulder flexion for each hand step and supports upper-body load
Keeps the elbow extended under load throughout the crawl
Holds the knees in the low hover (isometric knee extension against flexion) and drives each leg step
Anticipatory trunk bracing to keep the spine neutral during limb movement
Anti-extension control preventing lumbar sag in the hover position
Anti-rotation control as contralateral limbs leave the ground
Anti-rotation and pelvic control during the alternating limb pattern
Segmental lumbar stabilization while maintaining neutral spine
Co-contracts with the abdominals to hold neutral spine against flexion load
Frontal-plane pelvic control during single-leg support phases of the crawl
Resists lateral pelvic drop as each foot unweights
Produces hip flexion to bring each foot forward
Hip extension during push-off of the trailing leg
Assists arm loading and forward propulsion through the shoulder
Works with serratus anterior for scapular upward rotation and stability
Wrist stabilization/co-contraction in the extended, weight-bearing position
Wrist stabilization/co-contraction in the extended, weight-bearing position
Controls the loaded ankle with toes tucked under

Plantarflexors are actively load-bearing on the balls of the feet, co-contracting with tibialis anterior to stabilize the loaded ankle and assist forefoot push-off; not a passively stretched antagonist

cues.text: Imagine balancing a glass of water on your low back — do not let it spill as you crawl
Keep your knees hovering just an inch off the floor the entire time
Move the opposite hand and foot together — right hand with left foot
Take small, slow steps; if your hips sway or hike, shorten the step

regressions.name: Bear Hold
regressions.description: Hold the static bear position (knees hovering 2-5 cm) for 10-20 seconds without crawling, building the trunk endurance and wrist tolerance needed before adding limb movement.

regressions.name: Bear Position Shoulder Taps / Marching
regressions.description: From the bear hold, lift one hand to tap the opposite shoulder or lift one foot slightly, alternating sides without traveling — trains the anti-rotation demand with a smaller base-of-support challenge.

progressions.name: Multidirectional Bear Crawl
progressions.description: Crawl backward and laterally, not just forward; backward crawling increases the shoulder and quadriceps demand and the coordination challenge.

progressions.name: Loaded Bear Crawl (Plate Drag or Sled)
progressions.description: Crawl while dragging a weight plate under the torso or towing a light sled, or with a light load balanced on the back to enforce a level pelvis, adding resistance to the same pattern.

emgNotes: Direct EMG studies of the bear crawl itself are sparse; activation estimates are extrapolated from related quadruped and crawling-pattern research. Quadruped exercises with contralateral limb movement (e.g., bird-dog) show moderate activation of the multifidus, erector spinae, and abdominal obliques, and the bear crawl's reduced base of support and continuous limb exchange are expected to raise the anti-rotation demand on the obliques and transversus abdominis. Weight-bearing on the hands in a protracted, closed-chain position is a recognized stimulus for the serratus anterior, similar to push-up-plus variants. The hovering-knee position imposes a sustained isometric demand on the quadriceps and hip flexors that most plank-family exercises do not.
unmappedWanted: quadruped locomotion / crawl gait pattern (no whole-body locomotor movement exists in the movements roster)
finger flexors as a grouped 'wrist/hand stabilizers' concept for floor weight-bearing (individual forearm muscles exist but no group entry)

name: Chest Fly
description: Lie supine on a flat bench holding dumbbells above the chest with palms facing each other and a soft bend in the elbows. Lower the arms out to the sides in a wide arc until a gentle stretch is felt across the chest, then squeeze the arms back together over the chest along the same arc, keeping the elbow angle constant throughout.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover for shoulder horizontal adduction; loaded through a long arc including a stretched position at the bottom of the movement.
Assists horizontal adduction/flexion, especially through the top half of the arc.
Works isometrically to hold the fixed soft-elbow flexion angle against the load; not a meaningful contributor to horizontal adduction itself.
Stabilizes the scapula against the thorax as the arms move through the arc.
Anterior rotator cuff — resists anterior humeral head translation and maintains glenohumeral centration, particularly in the stretched bottom position.
Posterior rotator cuff — contributes to glenohumeral centration and stability during the loaded stretch.
Posterior rotator cuff — assists glenohumeral stabilization/centration through the arc.
Scapular retractor working isometrically to hold the set scapular position on the bench; not a stretched antagonist of the movement.
Scapular retractor stabilizing the retracted scapula against protraction, forming a force couple with serratus anterior.
Direct antagonist (horizontal abductor) of shoulder horizontal adduction; lengthens under control through the concentric phase.

cues.text: Imagine hugging a large barrel — keep that same gentle elbow bend the whole rep.
Lower until you feel a comfortable stretch across the chest, not pinching in the front of the shoulder.
Keep your shoulder blades pinched together and pressed into the bench from start to finish.
Bring the dumbbells together over the middle of your chest, not over your face or belly.

regressions.name: Floor Chest Fly
regressions.description: Perform the fly lying on the floor; the floor stops the arms at roughly neutral, limiting end-range shoulder stretch and reducing strain for beginners or those with anterior shoulder irritability.

regressions.name: Band Chest Fly (Standing)
regressions.description: Anchor a resistance band behind you and bring the arms together in a hugging arc; lighter, easily scalable resistance with a friendlier strength curve at end range.

progressions.name: Cable Fly
progressions.description: Perform the fly on a cable crossover; cables keep tension on the pectoralis major through the entire arc, including the fully adducted position where dumbbell torque drops off.

progressions.name: Slow-Eccentric Dumbbell Fly
progressions.description: Use a 3-4 second lowering phase into the stretched position to increase mechanical tension at long muscle length, a stimulus associated with hypertrophy in stretch-emphasized training.

dosing: 2-4 sets of 8-15 repetitions, 2x per week per general resistance-training guidelines for hypertrophy and strength; select a load leaving 1-3 reps in reserve. No fly-specific dosing trials exist.
emgNotes: Surface EMG comparisons of horizontal press and fly variations generally show high pectoralis major activation during the dumbbell fly, in a range comparable to the bench press at matched relative intensities, with substantially less triceps brachii involvement because the elbow angle is held constant. Anterior deltoid activity is moderate to high. The bottom (stretched) position places the pectoralis major under load at long muscle length, which biases mechanical tension there; it is also the position of greatest anterior shoulder stress, which motivates the limited-range floor variation for irritable shoulders. These are general findings from small EMG studies; the fly is far less studied than the bench press.
unmappedWanted: coracobrachialis (secondary horizontal adductor, not in roster)
pectoralis-minor (scapular stabilizer, not in roster)

name: Pec Deck
description: Sit tall on the machine with your back and head against the pad, feet flat, and forearms or hands on the lever pads with elbows at roughly chest height. Squeeze the pads together in front of your chest by horizontally adducting the shoulders, pause briefly at peak contraction, then slowly control the return until you feel a gentle stretch across the chest without letting the elbows drift behind the plane of the torso.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover for shoulder horizontal adduction; sternal and clavicular fibers both contribute through the arc.
Assists horizontal adduction, especially in the first half of the movement.
Short head crosses the glenohumeral joint anteriorly and weakly assists horizontal adduction; contribution is minor since the elbow rests passively on the pad and carries no flexion load.
Keeps the scapula stabilized against the thorax as the arms come together.
Anterior rotator cuff stabilization of the humeral head during the arc.
Posterior cuff co-contraction helps center the humeral head throughout the arc.
Direct antagonist (horizontal abductor); lengthens as the arms are drawn into horizontal adduction.
Lengthens with the relative scapular protraction at peak contraction; coach against losing scapular set entirely.
Elongates as the scapula protracts near peak; maintain eccentric control of scapular position.

cues.text: Squeeze the pads together like you are hugging a wide barrel.
Keep a soft bend in the elbows and your shoulder blades gently set against the back pad.
Lower slowly for 2-3 seconds and stop the stretch when your elbows reach the plane of your body.
Keep your chest tall and ribs down; do not shrug the shoulders toward your ears as you press in.

regressions.name: Limited-ROM pec deck
regressions.description: Set the machine's range limiter (or start the pads more forward) so the shoulders never move behind the frontal plane, reducing anterior capsule stress and load in the lengthened position.

regressions.name: Standing band chest fly
regressions.description: Perform horizontal adduction with a light resistance band anchored behind the body at chest height, allowing very light, easily graded resistance and free scapular movement.

progressions.name: Cable fly
progressions.description: Perform the fly on a cable crossover, which removes the fixed machine path and adds demand for shoulder and trunk stabilization through the arc.

progressions.name: Dumbbell fly on bench
progressions.description: Supine dumbbell fly requires the lifter to control the resistance arc and eccentric stretch without machine guidance, increasing stability and control demands.

dosing: 2-4 sets of 8-12 repetitions, 2-3 sessions per week, consistent with general resistance-training guidelines for hypertrophy and strength in healthy adults; load selected so the last 2-3 reps are challenging with good form.
emgNotes: Surface EMG comparisons of common chest exercises have reported pectoralis major activation on the pec deck that is comparable to the barbell bench press, with lower triceps involvement since the elbow is held in a fixed, slightly flexed position. Anterior deltoid is the main synergist; posterior cuff and scapular muscles work largely as stabilizers. Because the machine constrains the movement path, it is often used clinically to isolate horizontal adduction when free-weight stabilization demands are undesirable.
unmappedWanted: pectoralis-minor (would list as secondary/stabilizer for scapular anterior tilt)
coracobrachialis (weak synergist for horizontal adduction)

name: Hanging Leg Raise
description: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, shoulders actively packed (pulled down away from the ears). Keeping the knees straight or nearly straight, raise both legs to at least hip height, finishing by curling the pelvis posteriorly toward the ribs, then lower under control without swinging. The pelvic curl at the top is what shifts the demand from the hip flexors onto the abdominals.
bodyPosition: Vertical hang from overhead bar (open-chain lower body)

muscleRoles.notes: Main hip flexor driving the legs from full hang to hip height; works at long muscle length in the vertical hang.
Produces the posterior pelvic tilt and lumbar flexion at the top of the movement; also resists lumbar extension during the lowering phase.
Biarticular hip flexor; contributes to hip flexion when the knees are kept extended.
Assists posterior pelvic tilt and controls pelvic/trunk sway.
Assists posterior pelvic tilt and stabilizes the trunk against swing.
Assists hip flexion.
Assists hip flexion.
Assists early-range hip flexion.
Maintains intra-abdominal pressure and trunk stiffness during the hang.
Isometric shoulder extension/depression keeps the body from swinging in the hang.
Scapular depression for the active (packed) hang position.
Sustained isometric grip on the bar.
Sustained isometric grip on the bar.
Stretched over the extended knee and flexing hip; hamstring extensibility often limits straight-leg range.
Lengthens as the lumbar spine flexes and the pelvis posteriorly tilts at the top of each rep.

cues.text: Finish each rep by curling your pelvis up toward your ribs — lifting the legs alone is a hip-flexor exercise, not an ab exercise.
Pull your shoulders down away from your ears and hold that active hang throughout.
Imagine bringing your toes toward the bar, then lowering them slowly as if pressing against water.
Lower with control and pause briefly at the bottom of each rep so momentum and body swing never do the work.

regressions.name: Hanging Knee Raise
regressions.description: Perform the same movement with knees bent to 90 degrees, shortening the lever arm and reducing hip-flexor and grip demand while keeping the pelvic-curl pattern.

regressions.name: Reverse Crunch (Supine)
regressions.description: Lying supine, draw the knees toward the chest and lift the pelvis off the floor, training posterior pelvic tilt and lower-abdominal control without the grip and shoulder demands of the hang.

progressions.name: Toes-to-Bar
progressions.description: Increase range of motion by raising the straight legs all the way until the toes touch the bar, demanding greater abdominal shortening, hamstring flexibility, and lat control.

progressions.name: Weighted Hanging Leg Raise
progressions.description: Add load with ankle weights or a light dumbbell held between the feet, keeping strict form and controlled lowering.

dosing: 2-4 sets of 6-15 controlled repetitions, 2-3 sessions per week, consistent with general resistance-training progression guidelines; no exercise-specific dosing trials exist for this movement.
emgNotes: Comparative surface-EMG studies of abdominal exercises have measured hanging knee-up variations (including strap-supported versions) and generally report high rectus abdominis and oblique activity — among the highest of common ab exercises — along with substantial rectus femoris activity, reflecting the large hip-flexion component. Direct iliopsoas recordings are rare because the muscle is deep; its dominant role is inferred from biomechanics and fine-wire work on hip flexion tasks. Activation of the abdominals rises sharply when the pelvis is actively curled into posterior tilt at the top of the rep; without that curl the movement is predominantly hip-flexor driven with the abdominals working isometrically to fix the pelvis. Straight-leg variations are consistently more demanding than bent-knee versions due to the longer lever.

name: Cobra Pose
description: Lie prone with legs extended, tops of the feet on the mat, and palms flat under the shoulders with elbows tucked close to the ribs. Press the pubic bone and feet gently into the floor, then lift the chest by extending the spine — leading with the sternum rather than cranking the head back — using the back extensors first and the arms only to assist, keeping the elbows softly bent. Hold at a comfortable height with the shoulders drawn down and back, then lower slowly with control.

muscleRoles.notes: Concentric then isometric drivers of lumbar and thoracic extension against gravity
Segmental extension and control of the lumbar lordosis during the lift and hold; strongly active in prone trunk extension
Assists thoracic extension as the chest lifts
Assists via elbow extension when pressing through the hands; contribution grows the higher the variation
Depresses the scapulae, keeping shoulders away from the ears
Retracts the scapulae to open the chest
Works with middle trapezius for scapular retraction
Minor assist keeping the humerus extended and arms hugged to the trunk
Light engagement anchors the pelvis; over-gripping is usually discouraged to keep extension in the spine
Provide light hip extension to keep the pelvis grounded on the mat
Gentle abdominal support protects against uncontrolled hinging at a single lumbar segment
Stretched across the anterior trunk as the spine extends
Anterior fibers lengthen with trunk extension
Opens across the chest with scapular retraction and sternal lift
Anterior neck flexor lengthens if gentle cervical extension is added; keep the neck long rather than dropped back

cues.text: Press the tops of your feet and your pubic bone gently into the mat before you lift.
Lead with your breastbone, as if sliding your chest forward and up over a bar.
Draw your shoulder blades down and back, away from your ears.
Lift with your back muscles first — your hands should feel light enough to hover for a moment.

regressions.name: Sphinx Pose
regressions.description: Prop onto the forearms with elbows under the shoulders for a gentler, supported spinal extension with less lumbar load.

regressions.name: Baby Cobra
regressions.description: Lift the chest only a few inches with hands barely pressing, keeping the lower ribs on the mat to limit extension range.

progressions.name: Upward-Facing Dog
progressions.description: Straighten the elbows fully and lift the thighs and pelvis off the mat, supporting the body on the hands and tops of the feet for greater extension range and upper-body demand.

progressions.name: Active (Hands-Hovering) Cobra
progressions.description: At the top of the pose, lift the palms off the floor and hold, forcing the spinal extensors to sustain the full position without arm assistance.

emgNotes: Surface EMG work on prone trunk-extension exercises (including yoga-based studies of bhujangasana and studies of the closely related prone press-up used in McKenzie-style programs) consistently shows moderate activation of the lumbar erector spinae and multifidus, with activation rising as the chest is lifted higher and the arms contribute less. Middle/lower trapezius activity accompanies the scapular retraction-depression component, and triceps involvement scales with how much the hands press. Direct EMG data specific to cobra pose are sparse, so muscle roles here are inferred from prone-extension biomechanics and general yoga EMG literature rather than robust exercise-specific trials.
dosing: Commonly prescribed as 3-5 holds of 15-30 seconds, or 8-10 slow repetitions when performed dynamically like a press-up; this reflects clinical convention rather than trial-derived dosing.
unmappedWanted: longissimus/iliocostalis as separate segments (covered only by the erector-spinae group slug)
serratus posterior inferior
latissimus dorsi lengthening role was considered but omitted as clinically minor

name: Downward Dog (Adho Mukha Svanasana)
description: From hands-and-knees, tuck the toes and lift the hips up and back until the body forms an inverted V, arms and spine in one long line and knees softly extending. Press the floor away through spread fingers, reach the sit bones toward the ceiling, and let the heels sink toward the floor only as far as a neutral spine allows. Hold while breathing steadily, keeping the head relaxed between the upper arms.
bodyPosition: quadruped / inverted-V weight-bearing on hands and feet

muscleRoles.notes: Holds the shoulders in loaded overhead flexion against body weight in the closed-chain hold.
Drives scapular upward rotation and protraction; the 'press the floor away' action targets it.
Isometrically maintains elbow extension under body weight.
Assists upward rotation and keeps the scapulae depressed away from the ears.
Part of the upward-rotation force couple holding the scapula overhead (partly downregulated by the depression cue).
Active knee extension lifts the kneecaps, shares lower-limb load, and reciprocally deepens the hamstring stretch.
Centers the humeral head in weight-bearing shoulder flexion.
Maintains a long, neutral-to-slightly-extended spine against gravity.
Deep trunk control so the ribs don't flare in the inverted position.
Anterior trunk support limiting rib flare and lumbar hyperextension.
Gripping through the fingers protects the wrist in loaded extension.
Loaded stretch from combined hip flexion and knee extension; the usual limiter of the pose.
Stretched as heels reach toward the floor with knees extended (two-joint stretch).
Stretched in dorsiflexion, especially with bent-knee variations.
Elongated over the deeply flexed hip.
Stretched by full overhead shoulder flexion; tight lats show up as a rounded upper back here.
Sternal/sternocostal fibers lengthen at end-range overhead reach.

cues.text: Press the floor away from you and reach your hips up and back toward the wall behind you.
Spread your fingers wide and root down through the knuckles so the wrists share the load.
Bend your knees as much as you need to keep your back long — hips high beats heels down.
Let your head hang relaxed between your arms, ears in line with your upper arms.

regressions.name: Wall Downward Dog
regressions.description: Hands on a wall or countertop at hip height, hinge at the hips and walk back until the spine is long. Greatly reduces upper-limb load and hamstring demand; ideal for wrist pain, shoulder limitation, or contraindicated inversion.

regressions.name: Bent-Knee Downward Dog
regressions.description: Perform the full pose with knees generously bent and heels lifted, prioritizing a long spine and hip flexion over knee extension. Suits tight hamstrings and calves.

progressions.name: Three-Legged Dog
progressions.description: From the pose, extend one leg toward the ceiling while keeping the pelvis level. Adds single-limb load, hip-extensor work on the lifted side, and anti-rotation trunk demand.

progressions.name: Down Dog to Plank Flow / Pike Push-Up
progressions.description: Rhythmically shift between downward dog and high plank, or bend the elbows in the pike position and press back up. Adds dynamic serratus, deltoid, and triceps strengthening toward overhead-pressing capacity.

dosing: Hold 20-30 seconds (about 5 slow breaths) for 3-5 repetitions, most days of the week if used for hamstring/calf flexibility — consistent with general static-stretching dosage literature (30 s holds, repeated). No pose-specific dose-response trials exist, so strengthening-oriented flow dosing is by convention rather than evidence.
emgNotes: Small surface-EMG studies of hatha yoga poses (mostly in healthy adults, including older adults in the Yoga Empowers Seniors Study line of work) report low-to-moderate activation in downward dog: notable serratus anterior and trapezius activity from the weight-bearing upward-rotated scapula, moderate triceps and anterior deltoid activity, and modest erector spinae and abdominal activity for trunk control. Force-plate work shows roughly 55-65 percent of body weight borne through the upper limbs, which is why it is used to build wrist and shoulder weight-bearing tolerance. Hamstring and triceps surae activity is minimal — those muscles are primarily on stretch. Samples are small and protocols heterogeneous, so treat the numbers as indicative rather than definitive.
unmappedWanted: pectoralis-minor (stretched/recruited in scapular positioning; not in roster)

name: World's Greatest Stretch
description: A multi-segment dynamic mobility drill that chains a deep lunge, a hip/adductor opener, a thoracic rotation reach, and a hamstring lengthening in one flow. From standing, step into a long forward lunge and place both hands on the floor framing the lead foot. Drop the trailing knee toward the ground to load the trailing-side hip flexors. Bring the elbow on the lead-leg side down toward the instep to sink into the hip and groin. Then, keeping the outside hand planted, rotate the trunk and reach the lead-side arm up toward the ceiling, following the hand with the eyes to open the thoracic spine. Finally, shift the hips back and straighten the front knee to lengthen the hamstring, then return to the lunge and step back to stand. Move slowly and repeat, alternating sides.
bodyPosition: standing-to-lunge (forward lunge with hands on floor, transitioning to a half-kneeling rotational reach)

muscleRoles.notes: Drives the trunk/thoracic rotation of the reaching phase; contralateral external oblique pairs with the ipsilateral internal oblique to turn the ribcage toward the up-reaching arm.
Ipsilateral internal oblique co-produces the same rotation; also stabilizes the lumbopelvic region against the lunge base.
Deep segmental rotators assist and fine-tune the thoracic rotation as the chest opens.
Assists segmental rotation and extension of the spine during the open-up reach.
Contributes to thoracic extension/rotation and resists trunk flexion in the loaded lunge.
Reaching arm: assists horizontal abduction/extension as the hand travels up and back toward the ceiling.
Externally rotates the reaching shoulder to open the palm/chest at the top of the reach.
Assists external rotation of the reaching shoulder alongside infraspinatus.
Controls scapular upward rotation/depression of the reaching arm for a stable overhead position.
Retracts and stabilizes the scapula as the arm reaches back into rotation.
Provides intra-abdominal/lumbopelvic stiffness so rotation occurs at the thoracic spine rather than the lumbar spine.
Frontal-plane stabilization of the trunk over the split stance.
Stabilizes the lead-leg pelvis and controls femoral rotation in the deep lunge.
Eccentrically controls the lead-knee depth and supports the split-stance position; the trailing rectus femoris is also being stretched.
Stabilizes the scapula of the planted supporting arm and the reaching arm during upward rotation.
Trailing-leg hip flexor is stretched as that hip extends in the lunge — a primary mobility target of the drill.
Two-joint hip flexor/knee extensor of the trailing leg is lengthened by combined hip extension and knee flexion.
Lead-leg hamstrings are lengthened in the final phase when the front knee straightens and the hips shift back.
Lead-leg groin/adductors are stretched as the hip flexes, abducts, and externally rotates and the elbow drops toward the instep.
Lead-leg glute is placed on stretch in the deep hip-flexion of the lunge/elbow-to-instep position.
Lead-leg deep external rotator is lengthened in the flexed, externally rotated hip position (pigeon/figure-4-type stretch).
Reaching-side chest is opened and stretched as the arm rotates up and back.
Reaching-side lat is lengthened as the arm elevates and the thorax rotates open.

cues.text: Reach your top hand up to paint the ceiling and let your eyes follow the hand — this drives rotation from the mid-back, not the low back.
Plant the down-hand directly under your shoulder and keep it firmly on the floor as the anchor you rotate around.
Sink the hips down and forward first to feel a stretch across the front of the back hip before you rotate.
Move slowly and breathe out on the rotation — pause 1-2 seconds at the top rather than bouncing.

regressions.name: Half-kneeling rotation
regressions.description: Drop the trailing knee to the floor for a stable, supported base and perform only the thoracic rotation/reach, removing the balance and hamstring demands.

regressions.name: Elbow-to-instep only (no reach)
regressions.description: Hold the lunge with both hands down and rock gently into the hip/groin, omitting the overhead rotation until hip mobility improves.

progressions.name: Elevated rear foot / deeper load
progressions.description: Keep the trailing knee off the floor and press the hips lower to increase hip-flexor and adductor stretch and the balance demand.

progressions.name: Walking World's Greatest Stretch
progressions.description: Perform it moving forward one step at a time (walking lunge into rotation), turning it into a continuous dynamic warm-up flow that also challenges gait-pattern coordination.

dosing: Typically programmed as part of a dynamic warm-up: about 3-5 controlled repetitions per side, moving through the range with a brief 1-2 second pause at the rotational end range, for 1-2 rounds. These figures reflect common strength-and-conditioning practice rather than a specific validated protocol; as a dynamic mobility drill it is best done after a light general warm-up and before training, not held as a long static stretch.
emgNotes: There is no robust, exercise-specific EMG literature quantifying muscle activation for the World's Greatest Stretch, so the following is a biomechanical description rather than a measured activation profile. Actively, the drill demands low-to-moderate contraction of the trunk rotators — the obliques with assistance from the deep spinal rotators (rotatores, multifidus) and erector spinae — to produce and control the thoracic rotation, while the reaching shoulder recruits the posterior deltoid and external rotators (infraspinatus, teres minor) with lower/middle trapezius and serratus anterior stabilizing the scapula. The lumbopelvic stabilizers (transversus abdominis, quadratus lumborum) and lead-leg hip/thigh musculature (gluteus medius, quadriceps) work isometrically to hold the split-stance base. The primary intent, however, is passive/dynamic lengthening: the trailing-leg hip flexors (iliopsoas, rectus femoris), the lead-leg adductors, glutes, deep rotators (piriformis) and hamstrings, and the reaching-side pec major and latissimus dorsi are the targeted tissues placed on stretch.
